title: Non-Linear Mean Square Estimation
text:
The Philosophy: \(\theta\) is a fixed but unknown constant. There is no “probability” for \(\theta\); it just is what it is.
The Goal: Maximize the Likelihood Function \(f(x|\theta)\).
Process: We look for the value \(\hat{\theta}\) that satisfies: \[\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg \max_{\theta} L(\theta) = \arg \max_{\theta} \prod_{i=1}^{n} f(x_i | \theta)\] we solve: \[\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \ln L(\theta) = 0\]

Key Character: It only cares about the data you just measured. It doesn’t care about what you “expected” \(\theta\) to be before the experiment.




The Philosophy: \(\theta\) is a Random Variable. We have a “Prior” belief about it (\(f_{\theta}(\theta)\)) and we update that belief with data (\(f_{\theta|x}(\theta|x)\)).
Goal: Minimize the Mean Square Error (MSE): \[\min E[ (\hat{\theta}(x) - \theta)^2 ] = \min \iint (\hat{\theta}(x) - \theta)^2 f_{x,\theta}(x, \theta) \, dx \, d\theta\] Because \(f_{x,\theta}(x, \theta) = f_{\theta|x}(\theta|x) f_x(x)\): \[\min E[ (\hat{\theta}(x) - \theta)^2 ] = \min\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\hat{\theta}(x) - \theta)^2 f_{\theta|x}(\theta|x) \, d\theta \right] f_x(x) \, dx\] The outer integral is over \(x\), and the density \(f_x(x)\) is always non-negative. To minimize the total integral, we simply need to minimize the inner term (the term in the brackets) for every single specific value of \(x\). So, for a fixed \(x\), we want to find the value \(c\) (which represents our estimate \(\hat{\theta}(x)\)) that minimizes: \[J(c) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (c - \theta)^2 f_{\theta|x}(\theta|x) \, d\theta\] \[\frac{dJ}{dc} = \frac{d}{dc} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (c^2 - 2c\theta + \theta^2) f_{\theta|x}(\theta|x) \, d\theta = 0\] \[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (2c - 2\theta) f_{\theta|x}(\theta|x) \, d\theta = 0\] \[2c \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{\theta|x}(\theta|x) \, d\theta - 2 \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \theta f_{\theta|x}(\theta|x) \, d\theta = 0\] \[2c(1) - 2E[\theta|x] = 0\] \[c = \hat{\theta}_{MSE}(x) = E[\theta|x]\] \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = E[\theta | x] = \int \theta f_{\theta|x}(\theta|x) \, d\theta\]

Imagine the A-Posteriori distribution (the “Direction B” updated belief) is skewed:

MLE will point to the very highest peak (the most “likely” single value).
MSE will point to the center of mass (the “average” value). If the distribution is a perfect, symmetrical Gaussian Bell Curve, the peak (Mode) and the center (Mean) are the same. In that special case: \[\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \hat{\theta}_{MSE}\] But for almost any other distribution, they will give you different answers.





Prior Knowledge: We know from the manufacturer that the voltage is usually around \(10\text{V}\), modeled as \(\theta \sim N(10, 1)\).
The Measurement: We take one single, noisy measurement \(X\) with noise distribution \(N(0, 4)\)
Your Goal: You see a specific value \(X = 14\). You need to output an estimate \(\hat{\theta}\).



The MLE doesn’t care about the manufacturer’s manual. It only looks at the sensor.

Goal: Maximize \(f(X|\theta)\)
Logic: “I measured \(14\). The most likely value to produce a \(14\) is \(14\).” *Result: \[\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = x = 14\]



Principle: Maximize the Likelihood \(f(x|\theta)\) the measurement \(X\) are naturally assumed to be Gaussian distributed with \(\theta\) is \(N(\theta, \sigma_n^2)\). 1. Define the Joint PDF (Likelihood Function) We start with \(n\) independent observations \(X_i = \theta + w_i\), where the noise \(w_i\) follows a Normal distribution \(N(0, \sigma^2)\). This means each observation \(X_i\) is an independent Normal random variable \(X_i \sim N(\theta, \sigma^2)\). \[f_X(x_1, \dots, x_n; \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x_i - \theta)^2}{2\sigma^2}}\]

Convert to Log-Likelihood \[L(\theta) = \log \left( \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x_i - \theta)^2}{2\sigma^2}} \right)\] \[L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \log \left( \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \right) - \frac{(x_i - \theta)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right]\]
Take Derivative \[\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \text{constant} - \frac{(x_i - \theta)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right]\] \[\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{2(x_i - \theta)}{2\sigma^2} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \theta)\]
Set to Zero and Solve \[\frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \theta) = 0\] \[\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta = 0\] \[\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n\theta = 0\] \[\theta = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\]
Since we only observed one data point \(X = 14\), we have \(n=1\), so: \[\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{1} (14) = 14\]





The MSE approach uses the Prior information. \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = \frac{\sigma_n^2}{\sigma_p^2 + \sigma_n^2}\mu_p + \frac{\sigma_p^2}{\sigma_p^2 + \sigma_n^2}X\]
Or signal processing community also like the Kalman Filter style, like trust the prior first, then add the observed noise \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = \mu_p + K(X - \mu_p)\] Where the gain \(K\) is: \[K = \frac{\sigma_p^2}{\sigma_p^2 + \sigma_n^2}\]
Plugging in numbers (\(\mu_p = 10, \sigma_p^2 = 1, \sigma_n^2 = 4, X = 14\)): \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = \frac{4}{1+4}(10) + \frac{1}{1+4}(14)\] \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = 0.8(10) + 0.2(14) = 8 + 2.8 = 10.8\text{V}\]
Little Intuition:

Prior Trust: Because our sensor is quite noisy (\(\sigma_n^2=4\)) compared to our prior knowledge (\(\sigma_p^2=1\)), the MSE “trusts” the prior more. It pulls the measurement of 14 back towards the expected 10.
Limit Cases:

If \(\sigma_n^2 \ll \sigma_p^2\) (Clean Sensor): \(\hat{\theta}_{MSE} \approx X\). We trust the measurement completely.
If \(\sigma_n^2 \gg \sigma_p^2\) (Broken Sensor): \(\hat{\theta}_{MSE} \approx \mu_p\). We ignore the sensor and stick to our prior belief.




Principle: Find the Conditional Mean: \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = E[\theta|x] = \int \theta f(\theta|x) d\theta\] once we see data \(x\), the “new” distribution for our parameter is \(f(\theta|x)\), therefore the “new” mean (the best guess under MSE) is the average of all possible \(\theta\).
To do this, we first need the Posterior PDF \(f(\theta|x)\) using Bayes’ Theorem (Direction B): \[f(\theta|x) = \frac{f(x|\theta)f(\theta)}{f(x)}\]
Step 1: Multiply the Prior and Likelihood
Both are Gaussian, so the posterior is also Gaussian: \[f(\theta|x) \propto \exp \left( -\frac{(x-\theta)^2}{2\sigma_n^2} + -\frac{(\theta-\mu_p)^2}{2\sigma_p^2} \right)\]
The total exponent of the posterior is the sum: \[\text{Exponent} = -\frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{(\theta - \mu_p)^2}{\sigma_p^2} + \frac{(x - \theta)^2}{\sigma_n^2} \right]\]
Step 2: Complete the Square
In probability theory, when you multiply two Gaussians, the result is a new Gaussian. A Gaussian PDF always looks like \(e^{-\frac{(\theta - \mu_{new})^2}{2\sigma_{new}^2}}\).
Important, now we want to find the “center” (\(\mu_{new}\)) of this new bell curve. The term is a quadratic symmetric function of \(\theta\), and magically the center of the new bell curve is the same as the center of the original bell curve. So now we just need to find the center of the term/aka also the bell curve by finding the minimum of quadratic symmetric function: \[\text{Exponent} = -\frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{\theta^2 - 2\theta x + x^2}{\sigma_n^2} + \frac{\theta^2 - 2\theta \mu_p + \mu_p^2}{\sigma_p^2} \right]\] \[\text{Exponent} = -\frac{1}{2} \left[ \theta^2 \left( \frac{1}{\sigma_n^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_p^2} \right) - 2\theta \left( \frac{x}{\sigma_n^2} + \frac{\mu_p}{\sigma_p^2} \right) + \left( \frac{x^2}{\sigma_n^2} + \frac{\mu_p^2}{\sigma_p^2} \right) \right]\] Remember in secondary school, In any quadratic like \(A\theta^2 + B\theta + C\), the peak (the mean) is always at \(\theta = -B / 2A\):

\(A = \frac{1}{\sigma_n^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_p^2}\)
\(B = -2 \left( \frac{x}{\sigma_n^2} + \frac{\mu_p}{\sigma_p^2} \right)\) \[\mu_{new} = \frac{-B}{2A} = \frac{2 \left( \frac{x}{\sigma_n^2} + \frac{\mu_p}{\sigma_p^2} \right)}{2 \left( \frac{1}{\sigma_n^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_p^2} \right)} = \frac{\frac{x}{\sigma_n^2} + \frac{\mu_p}{\sigma_p^2}}{\frac{1}{\sigma_n^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_p^2}}\] Step 3: Solve the Integral Since we now know \(f(\theta|x)\) is a Gaussian with mean \(\mu_{new}\), the MSE integral becomes easy: \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \theta \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{new}^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(\theta - \mu_{new})^2}{2\sigma_{new}^2} \right) d\theta\] Let \(u = \theta - \mu_{new}\), which means \(d\theta = du\) and \(\theta = u + \mu_{new}\) \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (u + \mu_{new}) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{new}^2}} e^{-\frac{u^2}{2\sigma_{new}^2}} du\] \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{new}^2}} e^{-\frac{u^2}{2\sigma_{new}^2}} du}_{\text{Part A}} + \underbrace{\mu_{new} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{new}^2}} e^{-\frac{u^2}{2\sigma_{new}^2}} du}_{\text{Part B}}\]
Part A is 0: The integrand is an odd function (\(u\) is odd, the Gaussian part is even) integrated over a symmetric interval. It vanishes.
Part B is \(\mu_{new}\): The integral part is simply the area under a standard Gaussian curve, which is exactly 1. \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = 0 + \mu_{new} \cdot 1 = \mu_{new}\] The average of a Gaussian is simply its center point. Therefore: \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = \mu_{new} = \frac{\frac{1}{\sigma_p^2}\mu_p + \frac{1}{\sigma_n^2}x}{\frac{1}{\sigma_p^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_n^2}}\] \[\hat{\theta}_{MSE} = \frac{\frac{10}{1} + \frac{14}{4}}{\frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{4}} = \frac{10 + 3.5}{1.25} = \mathbf{10.8V}\]

title: Non-Linear Mean Square Estimation
text:
Foundations: Joint and Multivariate Densities
Given observations as a random vector \(X = [X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n]\), we define the joint probability density function (PDF) as: \[f(X) = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)\] \[P\{X \in D\} = \int_D f(X) dX\]
Example: \[P(20 \le X_1 \le 25, 30 \le X_2 \le 40) = \int_{30}^{40} \int_{20}^{25} f(x_1, x_2) \, dx_1 \, dx_2\]
Marginal density (PDF), just set others into from \(-\infty\) to \(+\infty\): \[f_{X_1}(x_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \dots \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \, dx_2 \dots dx_n\]

title: Non-Linear Mean Square Estimation
text:
Transformation of Random Variables
Given a random vector \(X = [X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n]\) and a set of \(k\) functions \(g_1, g_2, \dots, g_k\), we can form a new set of random variables: \[Y_1 = g_1(X), Y_2 = g_2(X), \dots, Y_k = g_k(X)\]
Our goal is to find the joint density \(f_y(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n)\),

First, write the transformation as: \[y_1 = g_1(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)\] \[y_2 = g_2(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)\] \[\vdots\] \[y_n = g_n(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)\]
Find the inverse transformation - aka solving the system above for \(x_i\) in terms of \(y_i\): \[x_1 = g_1^{-1}(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n)\] \[x_2 = g_2^{-1}(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n)\] \[\vdots\] \[x_n = g_n^{-1}(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n)\]
If there is a unique solution \(X = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]\), we use the multivariate version of the transformation formula: \[f_y(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n) = \frac{f_x(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)}{|J(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)|}\] where \(J\) is the Jacobian matrix of the transformation: \[J(x_1, \dots, x_n) = \begin{vmatrix} \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial x_1} & \dots & \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial x_n} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \frac{\partial g_n}{\partial x_1} & \dots & \frac{\partial g_n}{\partial x_n} \end{vmatrix}\]


Example: Polar Coordinates
transforming two i.i.d. Gaussian variables (\(X_1, X_2\)) into Polar coordinates (\(R, \Theta\)): #### 1. Identify the Relationships We start with the forward definitions for Polar coordinates:
\[y_1 = \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}\] (The distance from the origin \(r\)) \[y_2 = \arctan\left(\frac{x_2}{x_1}\right)\] (The angle \(\theta\))
Isolate the Ratio From the angle equation: \[ \tan(y_2) = \frac{x_2}{x_1} \implies x_2 = x_1 \tan(y_2) \]
Substitute into the Radius Equation Now plug \(x_2\) into the squared radius equation (\(y_1^2 = x_1^2 + x_2^2\)): \[ y_1^2 = x_1^2 + (x_1 \tan(y_2))^2 \] \[ y_1^2 = x_1^2 (1 + \tan^2(y_2)) \]
Use Trigonometric Identity Recall the identity \(1 + \tan^2(\theta) = \sec^2(\theta) = \frac{1}{\cos^2(\theta)}\): \[ y_1^2 = x_1^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\cos^2(y_2)} \]
Solve for \(x_1\) Move \(\cos^2(y_2)\) to the other side and take the square root: \[ x_1^2 = y_1^2 \cos^2(y_2) \implies \mathbf{x_1 = y_1 \cos(y_2)} \]
Solve for \(x_2\) Now go back to our substitution from Step 2: \[ x_2 = x_1 \tan(y_2) \] \[ x_2 = (y_1 \cos(y_2)) \cdot \frac{\sin(y_2)}{\cos(y_2)} \] \[ \mathbf{x_2 = y_1 \sin(y_2)} \]
This confirms that the inverse is simply the standard mapping from Polar \((r, \theta)\) to Cartesian \((x, y)\).

2. Jacobian
First, we find the derivatives of \(y_1\): \[\frac{\partial y_1}{\partial x_1} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}} \cdot 2x_1 = \frac{x_1}{\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}}\] \[\frac{\partial y_1}{\partial x_2} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}} \cdot 2x_2 = \frac{x_2}{\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}}\] Next, we find the derivatives of \(y_2\): \[\frac{\partial y_2}{\partial x_1} = \frac{1}{1 + (x_2/x_1)^2} \cdot \left(-\frac{x_2}{x_1^2}\right) = \frac{-x_2}{x_1^2 + x_2^2}\] \[\frac{\partial y_2}{\partial x_2} = \frac{1}{1 + (x_2/x_1)^2} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{x_1}\right) = \frac{x_1}{x_1^2 + x_2^2}\]
\[J(x_1, x_2) = \begin{vmatrix} \frac{\partial y_1}{\partial x_1} & \frac{\partial y_1}{\partial x_2} \\ \frac{\partial y_2}{\partial x_1} & \frac{\partial y_2}{\partial x_2} \end{vmatrix} = \begin{vmatrix} \frac{x_1}{\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}} & \frac{x_2}{\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}} \\ \frac{-x_2}{x_1^2 + x_2^2} & \frac{1}{x_1^2 + x_2^2} \end{vmatrix}\]
Determinant: \[|J(x_1, x_2)| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}}\] Since \(y_1 = \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}\), we have: \[|J(x_1, x_2)| = \frac{1}{y_1}\]


3. PDF
\[f_y(y_1, y_2) = \frac{f_x(x_1, x_2)}{|J(x_1, x_2)|} = \frac{f_x(y_1 \cos(y_2), y_1 \sin(y_2))}{1/y_1}\] \[f_y(y_1, y_2) = y_1 f_x(y_1 \cos(y_2), y_1 \sin(y_2))\]


4. Plug in Numbers
Assume \(X_1, X_2 \sim N(0, 1)\). Their individual probability density functions (p.d.f.) are: \[f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{x^2}{2}}\] Since they are independent, their joint p.d.f. is the product of their individual p.d.f.s: \[f_{X_1, X_2}(x_1, x_2) = \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{x_1^2}{2}} \right) \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{x_2^2}{2}} \right) = \frac{1}{2\pi} e^{-\frac{x_1^2 + x_2^2}{2}}\] We use the transformation \(x_1 = r \cos \theta\) and \(x_2 = r \sin \theta\).Substitute these into the exponent: \[x_1^2 + x_2^2 = (r \cos \theta)^2 + (r \sin \theta)^2 = r^2(\cos^2 \theta + \sin^2 \theta) = r^2\] Now, substitute this into the joint p.d.f. of \(X\): \[f_{X_1, X_2}(r \cos \theta, r \sin \theta) = \frac{1}{2\pi} e^{-\frac{r^2}{2}}\] Final: \[f_{R, \Theta}(r, \theta) = f_{X_1, X_2}(r \cos \theta, r \sin \theta) \cdot r\]\[f_{R, \Theta}(r, \theta) = \frac{r}{2\pi} e^{-\frac{r^2}{2}}\]
(optional)

Finding the Marginal Density of the Radius (R) To find the marginal density \(f_R(r)\), we integrate out the other variable, \(\theta\), over its entire range: \[f_R(r) = \int_{0}^{2\pi} f_{R, \Theta}(r, \theta) d\theta = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{r}{2\pi} e^{-\frac{r^2}{2}} d\theta\] Since the expression \(\frac{r}{2\pi} e^{-\frac{r^2}{2}}\) does not depend on \(\theta\), we can move it outside the integral: \[f_R(r) = \frac{r}{2\pi} e^{-\frac{r^2}{2}} \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta = \frac{r}{2\pi} e^{-\frac{r^2}{2}} \cdot [2\pi - 0]\]\[f_R(r) = r e^{-\frac{r^2}{2}}, \quad r \ge 0\]

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Stereo or LiDAR (ICP)
text:
Suppose we have a set of points \(\mathbf{P} = \{p_1, \dots, p_n\}\) and a second set of matched points \(\mathbf{P}' = \{p_1', \dots, p_n'\}\). We want to find the rotation \(\mathbf{R}\) and translation \(\mathbf{t}\) that best aligns \(\mathbf{P}'\) onto \(\mathbf{P}\).The error for a single pair \(i\) is: \[\mathbf{e}_i = p_i - (\mathbf{R}p_i' + \mathbf{t})\]
We want to minimize the sum of the squared errors for all \(n\) points: \[\min_{\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}} \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \|\mathbf{p}_i - (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i' + \mathbf{t})\|_2^2\]
Centroid Trick. Solving for \(\mathbf{R}\) and \(\mathbf{t}\) simultaneously is difficult because they are “tangled” together. To fix this, we calculate the centroids (the average position) of both point sets: \[\mathbf{p} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{p}_i, \quad \mathbf{p}' = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{p}_i'\]
Error after centroid trick: \[\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \|\mathbf{p}_i - (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i' + \mathbf{t})\|^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \|\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i' - \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}' + \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}'\|^2\] \[= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \|(\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R} (\mathbf{p}_i' - \mathbf{p}')) + (\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}' - \mathbf{t})\|^2\] \[= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\|\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R} (\mathbf{p}_i' - \mathbf{p}'))\|^2 + \|\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}' - \mathbf{t}\|^2 + 2(\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R} (\mathbf{p}_i' - \mathbf{p}'))^T (\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}' - \mathbf{t})).\]
\((\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R} (\mathbf{p}_i' - \mathbf{p}')) = 0\) because \(\sum (\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{p}) = 0\) (because \(\mathbf{p}\) is the average of \(\mathbf{p}_i\)) and \(\sum (\mathbf{p}_i' - \mathbf{p}') = 0\) (because \(\mathbf{p}'\) is the average of \(\mathbf{p}_i'\)). Therefore:
\[\min_{\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}} J = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \underbrace{\|\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}(\mathbf{p}_i' - \mathbf{p}')\|^2}_{\text{Term 1: Only depends on } \mathbf{R}} + \underbrace{\|\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}' - \mathbf{t}\|^2}_{\text{Term 2: Depends on both}}\]

Term 1 only cares about the shape and orientation of the point clouds, not where they are in the world. We can solve for \(\mathbf{R}\) here first.
Term 2 is trivial. Once we have \(\mathbf{R}\), we just pick a \(\mathbf{t}\) that makes this term zero. Specifically, \(\mathbf{t} = \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}'\).

To solve Term 1, we define “centered” coordinates (or de-centroid coordinates), effectively moving the center of both point clouds to the origin \((0,0,0)\):\[\mathbf{q}_i = \mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{p}\] \[\mathbf{q}_i' = \mathbf{p}_i' - \mathbf{p}'\]
The optimization for the rotation then becomes: \[\mathbf{R}^* = \arg \min_{\mathbf{R}} \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \|\mathbf{q}_i - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{q}_i'\|^2\] \[\mathbf{R}^* =\arg \min_{\mathbf{R}} \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{q}_i^T\mathbf{q}_i + \mathbf{q}_i'^T\mathbf{R}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{q}_i' - 2\mathbf{q}_i^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{q}_i')\]

\(\mathbf{q}_i^T\mathbf{q}_i\): This is just the length of the point from its own center. It doesn’t change when we rotate things. (Not relevant).
\(\mathbf{R}^T\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}\): Since \(\mathbf{R}\) is an orthogonal rotation matrix, this term simplifies to \(\mathbf{q}_i'^T\mathbf{q}_i'\), which is also constant.
The only term that changes when we change \(\mathbf{R}\) is the last one: \(-2\mathbf{q}_i^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{q}_i'\). To minimize a negative number, we must maximize the positive part. So the goal is now: \[\max_{\mathbf{R}} \sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf{q}_i^T \mathbf{R} \mathbf{q}_i'\]

A single dot product \(\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{b}\) can be written as the Trace (\(\text{tr}\)) of a matrix product. Summing all these dot products together allows us to pull the \(\mathbf{R}\) out of the summation: \[\sum \mathbf{q}_i^T \mathbf{R} \mathbf{q}_i' = \text{tr}\left( \mathbf{R} \sum \mathbf{q}_i' \mathbf{q}_i^T \right)\]
This is brilliant because it turns a “sum of many little problems” into a “single matrix problem.”
Now we solve \(\mathbf{R}\) by using SVD. Idk why but just see. We define a “Cross-Covariance” matrix \(\mathbf{W}\), which represents how the two point clouds are correlated in 3D space: \[\mathbf{W} = \sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i'^T\] We then perform SVD on this matrix: \[\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^T\]
Think of \(\mathbf{U}\) and \(\mathbf{V}\) as the “principal axes” of the two point clouds. To find the rotation that aligns these axes, the mathematical solution is simply: \[\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{V}^T\]


The goal is to maximize the alignment between two sets of points. We express this as the sum of their dot products: \(\sum \mathbf{q}_i^T (\mathbf{R} \mathbf{q}_i')\).We use two specific properties of the Trace (\(\text{tr}\)):

Scalar Property: A single number (like a dot product result) is equal to its own trace: \(\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{b} = \text{tr}(\mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{b})\) (1x1 matrix is still a matrix, this is exactly the trick!!!)
Cyclic Property: You can move matrices around in a circle inside a trace: \(\text{tr}(AB) = \text{tr}(BA)\).

Let’s apply this to one pair of points: \[\mathbf{q}_i^T (\mathbf{R} \mathbf{q}_i') = \text{tr}(\mathbf{q}_i^T \mathbf{R} \mathbf{q}_i')\] Now, “cycle” \(\mathbf{q}_i^T\) to the back: \[= \text{tr}(\mathbf{R} \mathbf{q}_i' \mathbf{q}_i^T)\] Because the Trace of a sum is the same as the sum of the Traces (\(\sum \text{tr} = \text{tr} \sum\)), we can pull the constant rotation \(\mathbf{R}\) and the Trace operator out of the summation:\[\sum \text{tr}(\mathbf{R} \mathbf{q}_i' \mathbf{q}_i^T) = \text{tr}\left( \mathbf{R} \sum \mathbf{q}_i' \mathbf{q}_i^T \right)\]

SO this is exactly the trick converting single vectors problem to the whole matrix

The dot product \(\mathbf{q}_i^T \mathbf{R} \mathbf{q}_i'\) measures how much point \(A\) and point \(B\) “agree” on their direction.

Without the trick: You are asking 100 individual questions: “Does point 1 line up? Does point 2 line up?”
With the trick: You condense all the relative directions into one single matrix \(\mathbf{W} = \sum \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i'^T\). This matrix is like a “summary” of how the two point clouds are oriented relative to each other. The Trace then measures the total “agreement” between \(\mathbf{R}\) and that summary.




Why \(\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{V}^T\)? This is known as the Orthogonal Procrustes Problem. Once we have our summary matrix \(\mathbf{W}\), we want to find the rotation \(\mathbf{R}\) that makes \(\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{W})\) as large as possible.
Think of the SVD \(\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T\) like this:

\(\mathbf{V}\): “Principal Axes” (the main directions) of the first point cloud.
\(\mathbf{U}\): “Principal Axes” of the second point cloud.
\(\mathbf{\Sigma}\): How strongly the features in the first cloud line up with features in the second.

If you want to align two objects, the most logical thing to do is align their axes. Multiplying \(\mathbf{U}\) by \(\mathbf{V}^T\) essentially creates a map that says: “Take the X-axis of cloud A and point it toward the X-axis of cloud B.”
So to formalize, we want to maximize \(\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T)\). Using the cyclic property again, this is \(\text{tr}(\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{U})\).Let’s define a new matrix \(\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{V}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{U}\).Since \(\mathbf{V}, \mathbf{R}, \text{and } \mathbf{U}\) are all orthogonal matrices, \(\mathbf{Z}\) must also be an orthogonal matrix.The maximum possible value for any entry in an orthogonal matrix is \(1\).To maximize the trace (the sum of the diagonal), we want \(\mathbf{Z}\) to be the Identity Matrix (\(\mathbf{I}\)).If \(\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{I}\), then: \[\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{V}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{U}\] \[\mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}\mathbf{U}^T = \mathbf{R}\] \[\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{V}^T\]
Why do we ignore \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) for Rotation? If I want to tell you how to rotate a football to match another football, I only need to tell you which way the “long ends” should point (\(\mathbf{U}\) and \(\mathbf{V}\)). I don’t care how long the football actually is (\(\mathbf{\Sigma}\)).
What exactly is \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\)? Think of \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) as a measure of “Common Signal Strength.”. \(\sigma_i\) is effectively the sum of the projections of the points onto those axes.\[\sigma_i = \sum (\text{Point } i \text{'s position on axis } v) \cdot (\text{Point } i \text{'s position on axis } u)\] If both clouds have points very far out on their respective axes, \(\sigma\) is huge. If one cloud is “flat” in a direction where the other is “tall,” \(\sigma\) will be tiny.

text:

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Monocular Camera
text:
Goal: estimate camera motion based on adjacent images. Based on landmarks/image features. It could be corners (Harris, FAST), blobs (SIFT, SURF, ORB), or edges. Extracting about 1000 feature points in the same image takes about 15.3ms for ORB, 217.3ms for SURF, and 5228.7ms for SIFT. It can be seen that ORB made a significant boost in speed while maintaining the features of rotation and scale invariance


the extraction of ORB features is divided into the following two steps:

original FAST corner detector has a major weakness: it is rotation-invariant. If you rotate the camera, the corner looks different to the computer, and it can’t match it anymore. Therefore, ORB finds the “orientation” of each corner from a small circle around the corner \[m_{pq} = \sum_{x,y \in B} x^p y^q I(x,y) \quad p,q \in \{0,1\}\]

\(I(x,y)\): The brightness (gray value) of the pixel at coordinates \((x,y)\).
\(m_{00}\): The total “mass” (sum of all pixel values). Not multiply by any coordinate
\(m_{10}\) and \(m_{01}\): The weighted sums in the \(x\) and \(y\) directions. Basically multiply by its x or y coordinate.

The Centroid is the “center of brightness” of the patch. \[C = \left( \frac{m_{10}}{m_{00}}, \frac{m_{01}}{m_{00}} \right)\]
Now we have two points:

\(O\): The geometric center of the patch (where the corner was detected).
\(C\): The intensity centroid (the “center of mass”).

So we get a vector \(\vec{OC}\). The angle of this vector tells us which way the “brightness” is leaning: \[\theta = \arctan(m_{01} / m_{10})\]
BRIEF descriptor:

We pre-define a set of \(n\) pairs of coordinates \((p, q)\) relative to a center point. Usually, \(n = 128, 256, \text{ or } 512\).
We used the angle \(\theta\) calculated in the previous step to rotate these pairs.
For each pair, we perform a simple test \(\tau\): \[\tau(p, q) =
  \begin{cases}
  1 & \text{if } I(p) > I(q) \
  0 & \text{otherwise}
  \end{cases}\]
We string all these 1s and 0s together to form a descriptor vector \(\mathbf{d}\)
Compare (e.g. 1011 XOR 1010 = 0001 - very small distance, so maybe same feature)






If monocular => 2 sets of 2D points => solved by epipolar geometry
If stereo => 2 sets of 3D points => solved by ICP
If one set is 3D and one set is projected 2D => PnP

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Monocular Camera
text:
If monocular => 2 sets of 2D points => solved by epipolar geometry
If stereo => 2 sets of 3D points => solved by ICP
If one set is 3D and one set is projected 2D => PnP

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Monocular Camera
text:
Epipolar Constraint (Essential Matrix)
Firstly, defining the 3D point \(P\) in world coordinates: \[\mathbf{P} = [X, Y, Z]^T\]
Assume the first camera lies on origin world (0,0,0) (no external). Relationship between a 3D point \(\mathbf{P}\) and its pixel locations \(\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2\): \[\mathbf{p}_1 \simeq \mathbf{K P}, \quad \mathbf{p}_2 \simeq \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{R P} + \mathbf{t})\]
Now normalized camera coordinates of point \(P\) in each camera \(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2\): \[\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{p}_1 = \mathbf{P}, \quad \mathbf{x}_2 = \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{p}_2 = \mathbf{R P} + \mathbf{t}\]
Substituting these into the relationship, we get: \[\mathbf{x}_2 \simeq \mathbf{R} \mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{t}\]
Let’s apply a cross product of a vector that is perpendicular to the plane containing \(\mathbf{t}\) and \(\mathbf{x}_2\) (which is written as skew-symmetric matrix \(\mathbf{t}^\wedge\)): \[\mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{x}_2 \simeq \mathbf{t}^\wedge (\mathbf{R} \mathbf{x}_1 + \mathbf{t})\] \[\mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{x}_2 \simeq \mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{R} \mathbf{x}_1 + \underbrace{\mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{t}}_{\mathbf{0}}\] \[\mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{x}_2 \simeq \mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{R} \mathbf{x}_1\]
Left multiply by \(\mathbf{x}_2^T\) to reveal the magic: \[\mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{x}_2 \simeq \mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{R} \mathbf{x}_1\]
Because \(\mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{x}_2\) is a vector perpendicular to both \(\mathbf{t}\) and \(\mathbf{x}_2\), the dot product \(\mathbf{x}_2^T (\mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{x}_2)\) is strictly zero. This gives us the concise Epipolar Constraint: \[\mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{R} \mathbf{x}_1 = 0\] \[\mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{E} \mathbf{x}_1 = 0\]
where \(\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}\) is the essential matrix.
Or if you want to work directly with \(\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2\): \[\mathbf{p}_2^T \mathbf{K}^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{p}_1 = 0\] \[\mathbf{p}_2^T \mathbf{F} \mathbf{p}_1 = 0\]
where \(\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1}\) is the fundamental matrix.
OK, now that means, we need to find \(\mathbf{t}\) and \(\mathbf{R}\) from the essential matrix \(\mathbf{E}\).

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Monocular Camera
section: Find R and t from Essential Matrix
text:
Find R and t from Essential Matrix
Now that we have the theory (\(\mathbf{x}_2^T \mathbf{E} \mathbf{x}_1 = 0\)), we need to solve for the 9 numbers inside \(\mathbf{E}\). It has the following properties:

Scale Equivalence: Because the equation equals zero, multiplying \(\mathbf{E}\) by any number (like 2 or 10) doesn’t change anything. This means we can only determine \(\mathbf{E}\) up to a scale.
Singular Values: Singular values must be \([\sigma, \sigma, 0]^T\). The first two are equal, and the last is zero. This is a consequence of \(\mathbf{E}\) being built from a rotation and a cross-product matrix.
5 Degrees of Freedom (DoF): Even though it has 9 slots, it only has 5 independent “moving parts” (3 for rotation, 3 for translation, minus 1 for the scale we can’t know)


Solving with 8-Point Algorithm
Since \(\mathbf{E}\) has 5 DoF, you might think we only need 5 pairs of points. However, the math for a 5-point solution is incredibly complex (nonlinear).Instead, we use the Eight-Point Algorithm. It treats the 9 elements of \(\mathbf{E}\) as independent linear variables and assuming the linear algebra estimation is good enough. But actually in SLAM, we have more than 8 points, so we can use the least square method to solve for \(\mathbf{E}\).
Take a pair of matched points in normalized coordinates and put them into the epipolar constraint equation: \(\mathbf{x}_1 = [u_1, v_1, 1]^T\) and \(\mathbf{x}_2 = [u_2, v_2, 1]^T\).
\[(u_2, v_2, 1) \begin{pmatrix} e_1 & e_2 & e_3 \\ e_4 & e_5 & e_6 \\ e_7 & e_8 & e_9 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} u_1 \\ v_1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = 0\]
Expanding this out, we get a linear equation with 9 unknowns (the elements of \(\mathbf{E}\)): \[u_1 u_2 e_1 + v_1 u_2 e_2 + u_2 e_3 + u_1 v_2 e_4 + v_1 v_2 e_5 + v_2 e_6 + u_1 e_7 + v_1 e_8 + e_9 = 0\] Or in matrix form: \[[u_2u_1, u_2v_1, u_2, v_2u_1, v_2v_1, v_2, u_1, v_1, 1] \cdot \mathbf{e} = 0\] where \(\mathbf{e} = [e_1, e_2, \dots, e_9]^T\) is a vector containing all 9 elements of the matrix \(\mathbf{E}\).
By taking 8 pairs of matched points, we create a giant \(8 \times 9\) matrix. Each row represents one matched pair \((u_1, v_1) \leftrightarrow (u_2, v_2)\). \[\begin{pmatrix} u_2^1 u_1^1 & u_2^1 v_1^1 & u_2^1 & v_2^1 u_1^1 & v_2^1 v_1^1 & v_2^1 & u_1^1 & v_1^1 & 1 \\ u_2^2 u_1^2 & u_2^2 v_1^2 & u_2^2 & v_2^2 u_1^2 & v_2^2 v_1^2 & v_2^2 & u_1^2 & v_1^2 & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ u_2^8 u_1^8 & u_2^8 v_1^8 & u_2^8 & v_2^8 u_1^8 & v_2^8 v_1^8 & v_2^8 & u_1^8 & v_1^8 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} e_1 \\ e_2 \\ e_3 \\ e_4 \\ e_5 \\ e_6 \\ e_7 \\ e_8 \\ e_9 \end{pmatrix} = \mathbf{0}\]

Logic: \(\mathbf{e}\) is lying in the null space of this coefficient matrix (definition \(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}\))
Scale Equivalence: Since the right side is \(\mathbf{0}\), \(\mathbf{e}\) can only be solved “up to a scale.” If the matrix has a rank of 8, the null space has a dimension of 1 (a line), which perfectly matches this scale ambiguity. Because if \(\mathbf{e}\) is a solution, then any multiple of it (\(2\mathbf{e}\), \(-5\mathbf{e}\), \(0.3\mathbf{e}\)) is also a solution.
Example: It is like Moving 1 meter and seeing a house 10 meters away is the same as Moving 10 meters and seeing a giant house 100 meters away.



Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
Any linear transformation (represented by a matrix \(\mathbf{A}\)) can be broken down into three simple, sequential geometric steps: Rotate, Stretch, and Rotate again.

\(\mathbf{V}^T\) (First Rotation): You rotate the circle so its axes align with the “stretching” directions of the matrix.
\(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) (Stretching): You pull or squish the circle into an ellipse. The singular values (\(\sigma_1, \sigma_2\)) (example in 2D) tell you exactly how long the axes of that ellipse are.
\(\mathbf{U}\) (Second Rotation): You rotate the resulting ellipse into its final orientation in the output space.

When we say \(\mathbf{e}\) is in the “null space,” we are looking for a direction that the matrix \(\mathbf{A}\) “squishes” down to zero. If the last singular value \(\sigma_9\) is zero, it means the transformation represented by \(\mathbf{A}\) completely collapses any vector pointing in the direction of the last column of \(\mathbf{V}\).
To find the best approximate solution that satisfies the geometric constraints, we use Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). Any matrix \(\mathbf{A}\) can be decomposed as: \[\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^T\]
So our problem is now: \[\min_{\|\mathbf{e}\|=1} \|\mathbf{Ae}\|^2\] \[\|\mathbf{Ae}\|^2 = \|\mathbf{U\Sigma V}^T \mathbf{e}\|^2 = \|\mathbf{\Sigma V}^T \mathbf{e}\|^2\]

\(\mathbf{U}\) (Left Singular Vectors): An \(m \times m\) orthogonal matrix. The columns of \(\mathbf{U}\) are the orthoginal eigenvectors of symmetric \(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^T\). Bringing eigenvector back to the original space.
\(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) (Singular Values): An \(m \times n\) diagonal matrix. The entries \(\sigma_i\) are the square roots of the eigenvalues of \(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\). Scaling factors along the principal axes. This also contain dimension adder/reducer, \(\mathbf{\Sigma}\) has actually same shape with \(\mathbf{A}\). In Essential Matrix calculation, a zero singular value means one dimension has no strength—it’s the direction where everything maps to zero.
\(\mathbf{V}^T\) (Right Singular Vectors): An \(n \times n\) orthogonal matrix. The columns of \(\mathbf{V}\) are the orthoginal eigenvectors of symmetric \(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A}\). Bringing original base vector to the eigenvectors. The very last column of \(\mathbf{V}\) (associated with the smallest \(\sigma\)) is the direction that is “least changed” (or most collapsed) by \(\mathbf{A}\).

For the essential matrix, the theoretical singular values are \([\sigma, \sigma, 0]^T\). In practice, due to noise, we get something like \([\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3]^T\) where \(\sigma_1 \approx \sigma_2\) and \(\sigma_3\) is very small but not exactly zero.
The solution \(\mathbf{e}\) that minimizes the error \(|\mathbf{A} \mathbf{e}|\) is the eigenvector corresponding to the smallest singular value. In the SVD decomposition, this corresponds to the last column of \(\mathbf{V}\) (or the last row of \(\mathbf{V}^T\)).
Explain THE LOGIC: The last column of \(\mathbf{V}\) is the “least powerful” direction of the matrix \(\mathbf{A}\). Since we want \(\mathbf{Ae} \approx 0\), we pick the direction that \(\mathbf{A}\) has the least power over! With the weighting of 1. So, we extract \(\mathbf{e}\) from the last column of \(\mathbf{V}\) and reshape it back into a \(3 \times 3\) matrix to get our estimated essential matrix \(\mathbf{E}_{est}\).


Recovering Rotation and Translation
Once we have \(\mathbf{E}_{est}\), we can decompose it to find the rotation \(\mathbf{R}\) and translation \(\mathbf{t}\). The decomposition is: \[\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}\]
Using SVD on \(\mathbf{E}_{est}\): \[\mathbf{E}_{est} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^T\]
We can derive the possible solutions for \(\mathbf{R}\) and \(\mathbf{t}\):

Rotation Matrix \(\mathbf{R}\): We know \(\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{R}\). There is a mathematical identity (believe me its out of scope of this blog) that says a skew-symmetric matrix \(\mathbf{t}^\wedge\) (with unit length) can be written as:\[\mathbf{t}^\wedge = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{W} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{U}^T \quad \text{or} \quad \mathbf{t}^\wedge = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{U}^T\]

Therefore: \[\mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^T = \mathbf{E} = \mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{W} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{U}^T \mathbf{R}\]
So:
\[\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{W} \mathbf{V}^T \quad \text{or} \quad \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{V}^T\]
where \(\mathbf{W} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\) (a rotation by 90 degrees around the z-axis).

Translation Vector \(\mathbf{t}\):

By definition of the SVD, the columns of \(\mathbf{U}\) represent the principal directions of the output space (the range of the transformation). Because the Essential Matrix has a rank of 2 (due to the singular values \([\sigma, \sigma, 0]\)), it “squashes” one dimension to zero.In \(\mathbf{V}^T\), the last column is the direction that gets squashed (the null space of \(\mathbf{E}\)).In \(\mathbf{U}\), the last column (\(\mathbf{u}_3\)) is the direction that cannot be reached by the transformation.
In the specific case of \(\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{t}^\wedge \mathbf{R}\), the “direction” that defines the transformation’s structure is the translation \(\mathbf{t}\). Because \(\mathbf{t}^\wedge\) is a cross product with \(\mathbf{t}\), the output of \(\mathbf{t}^\wedge\) is always perpendicular to \(\mathbf{t}\). Therefore, \(\mathbf{t}\) is the “missing” direction in the output, which corresponds exactly to the third column of \(\mathbf{U}\).
\[\mathbf{t} = \mathbf{U} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \quad \text{or} \quad \mathbf{t} = -\mathbf{U} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\]
This gives us four possible pairs of \((\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})\). After that we test all 4 pairs with the condition that the 3D points should be in front of the camera (positive \(Z\) coordinate in camera frame) using Triangulation.

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Monocular Camera
text:
Formulation H
The above Essential matrix can be used in any case. But for features that we are sure they are on a planar surface (e.g. floor, wall, table, etc.), we can use Homography to estimate the relative pose of the camera.
We have 3D points \(\mathbf{P}\) lie on a plane. In 3D space, a plane is defined by its normal vector \(\mathbf{n}\) and its distance to the origin \(d\): \[\mathbf{n}^T \mathbf{P} + d = 0\] \[-\frac{\mathbf{n}^T \mathbf{P}}{d} = 1\]
Let’s derive!!! Now, we look at how the point \(\mathbf{P}\) projects into our two cameras. Recall the general projection formula for the second camera: \[\mathbf{p}_2 \simeq \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{t})\]
Now as we know all points are on the same plane, we are allowed to do this: \[\mathbf{p}_2 \simeq \mathbf{K}\left(\mathbf{R}\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{t} \cdot \left(-\frac{\mathbf{n}^T \mathbf{P}}{d}\right)\right)\] \[\mathbf{p}_2 \simeq \mathbf{K}\left(\mathbf{R} - \frac{\mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^T}{d}\right) \mathbf{P}\] Finally, we want the relationship between pixels in Image 1 (\(\mathbf{p}_1\)) and pixels in Image 2 (\(\mathbf{p}_2\)). Since \(\mathbf{p}_1 \simeq \mathbf{K}\mathbf{P}\), we can say \(\mathbf{P} \simeq \mathbf{K}^{-1}\mathbf{p}_1\), we can substitue: \[\mathbf{p}_2 \simeq \underbrace{\mathbf{K}\left(\mathbf{R} - \frac{\mathbf{t}\mathbf{n}^T}{d}\right) \mathbf{K}^{-1}}_{\mathbf{H}} \mathbf{p}_1\]

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Monocular Camera
text:
Let’s solve H
So now we have: \[\mathbf{p}_2 \simeq \mathbf{H}\mathbf{p}_1\] \[\begin{pmatrix} u_2 \\ v_2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \simeq \begin{pmatrix} h_1 & h_2 & h_3 \\ h_4 & h_5 & h_6 \\ h_7 & h_8 & h_9 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} u_1 \\ v_1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\]
Because the relationship is “up to a scale,” we are allowed to divide the first two rows by the third row. \[u_2 = \frac{h_1 u_1 + h_2 v_1 + h_3}{h_7 u_1 + h_8 v_1 + h_9}\] \[v_2 = \frac{h_4 u_1 + h_5 v_1 + h_6}{h_7 u_1 + h_8 v_1 + h_9}\] By setting \(h_9 = 1\) (normalizing the matrix), we have: \[h_1 u_1 + h_2 v_1 + h_3 - h_7 u_1 u_2 - h_8 v_1 u_2 = u_2\] \[h_4 u_1 + h_5 v_1 + h_6 - h_7 u_1 v_2 - h_8 v_1 v_2 = v_2\]
Since one pair of points provides 2 constraints, and the homography matrix has 8 degrees of freedom (9 elements minus 1 for scale), we need at least 4 pairs of matched points to solve for \(\mathbf{H}\). \[\begin{pmatrix} u_1^1 & v_1^1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -u_1^1 u_2^1 & -v_1^1 u_2^1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & u_1^1 & v_1^1 & 1 & -u_1^1 v_2^1 & -v_1^1 v_2^1 \\ u_1^2 & v_1^2 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -u_1^2 u_2^2 & -v_1^2 u_2^2 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & u_1^2 & v_1^2 & 1 & -u_1^2 v_2^2 & -v_1^2 v_2^2 \\ u_1^3 & v_1^3 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -u_1^3 u_2^3 & -v_1^3 u_2^3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & u_1^3 & v_1^3 & 1 & -u_1^3 v_2^3 & -v_1^3 v_2^3 \\ u_1^4 & v_1^4 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -u_1^4 u_2^4 & -v_1^4 u_2^4 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & u_1^4 & v_1^4 & 1 & -u_1^4 v_2^4 & -v_1^4 v_2^4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} h_1 \\ h_2 \\ h_3 \\ h_4 \\ h_5 \\ h_6 \\ h_7 \\ h_8 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} u_2^1 \\ v_2^1 \\ u_2^2 \\ v_2^2 \\ u_2^3 \\ v_2^3 \\ u_2^4 \\ v_2^4 \end{pmatrix}\]
We find \(\mathbf{H}\) by solving this linear equation.
After that, we get \(\mathbf{R}\) and \(\mathbf{t}\) also from SVD.
And eventually also choose 1 of 4 possible solutions using cheirality check.
Well, in real SLAM, I think people do both F and H estimation and choose the best one (smaller error).

title: X, Us, CT, MRI
text:
Attenuation increased with atomic number and density. \[N = N_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \cdot d}\] different body parts have different attenuation coefficients (\(\mu\)). For example, bone (Calcium, \(Z=20\)) has a very high \(\mu\) because it is dense, while lung tissue has a very low \(\mu\) because it is mostly air.





No Absorption/No Ionization
The X-ray photon passes through the material without interacting and no ionization.





Maximum Photoelectric Absorption
The photon’s energy is just right to be completely absorbed by the inner-shell electron (e.g., K-shell). The electron is ejected as a high-speed photoelectron (\(\text{KE} \approx 0\) since \(E_x \approx P\)), leaving a positive ion with an unstable core hole.
The X-ray beam is highly attenuated (absorbed).







Photoelectric Absorption + Compton Scattering
The photon has enough energy to eject an inner-shell electron, but the remaining energy is re-emitted as a characteristic X-ray (fluorescence) or causes an Auger electron to be ejected.
attenuation but a bit of scattering





Compton Scattering Dominates
The photon loses only small part of its energy for absorption (it does not disappear) and is scattered in a new direction with reduced energy and longer wavelength.
image blur


Just Right!!! \(E_x \approx P\) is the best for stopping the photon.

Pros:

cheap
Fast
High contrast
Good for bone

Cons:

Ionizing radiation: The high-speed ejected electron (the photoelectron) then collides with surrounding atoms and molecules like DNA strands, breaking its chemical bonds

title: X, Us, CT, MRI
text:
Procedure
Estimation at each projection (angle) \(j\): \[\hat{P}_j = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} l_{ij} \cdot \mu_i\] therefore: \[\mu_i = \frac{P_j}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} l_{ij}}\]
For each projection (angle) \(j\), we calculate the difference between the measured projection \(P_j\) and the estimated projection \(\hat{P}_j\): \[\Delta_j = P_j - \hat{P}_j\]
Then we update the estimated attenuation coefficients \(\mu_i\) by adding a fraction of the difference: \[\mu_i = \mu_i + \frac{1}{N} \Delta_j\]
Repeat this process until the difference between the measured and estimated projections is minimized.

title: X, Us, CT, MRI
text:
Main Goal
Deconstructing the Magnetic Moment (Longitudinal \(M_z\) vs. Transverse \(M_{xy}\)) and what we eventually measure is \(M_{xy}\). But the problem is that each hydrogen proton (though same frequency) has different phase, so the net \(M_{xy}\) is 0.
Therefore now we need to make the phase of all hydrogen protons in the same direction. We do this by applying a Radio Frequency (RF) pulse perpendicular to \(B_0\) of the same Larmor frequency (\(\omega_0\)). 2 Effects:

It forces all the randomly dispersed wobbling protons together to have the same phase position and perfectly lying flat into the \(x,y\) plane. => Therefore individual transverse magnetization vectors add up to create a measurable net transverse magnetization (\(M_{XY}\)).
This extra energy forces some of the “Spin Up” (low energy) protons to flip into the “Spin Down” (high energy) state. => Longitudinal magnetisation is now zero.

Before RF Pulse (Slide: Longitudinal Magnetisation):

The vector points straight up.
Longitudinal magnetisation is at its maximum.
Transverse magnetisation is zero.

During a 90° RF Pulse (Slide: Transverse Magnetisation, \(\alpha=90^\circ\)):

enough energy to flip the vector perfectly flat into the \(x,y\) plane.
The longitudinal magnetisation is now zero
The transverse magnetisation is now equal to the original longitudinal magnetisation (maximum transverse magnetisation)

This rotating \(M_{XY}\) vector is a moving magnetic field. According to Faraday’s law of induction, this moving magnetic field induces an electrical current in the scanner’s receiver coils. This electrical current is the MRI signal.

title: X, Us, CT, MRI
text:
Relaxation
The first slide establishes the basic idea: once the 90° RF pulse is gone, the protons don’t stay flipped down in the transverse plane. They want to return to their lowest energy state—their normal equilibrium aligned with the main \(B_0\) field. 2 things happening:

T1 Relaxation (Spin-Lattice Relaxation): The protons release their extra energy to the surrounding “lattice” (the molecules in the tissue). This causes the longitudinal magnetization (\(M_z\)) to gradually recover.
T2 Relaxation (Spin-Spin Relaxation): The protons lose their synchronized phase coherence due to interactions with each other. This causes the transverse magnetization (\(M_{xy}\)) to decay.

Let’s focus on T1 Relaxation (Spin-Lattice Relaxation) first. During the RF pulse, many protons were forced into the high-energy “Spin Down” state. To get back to the low-energy “Spin Up” state, they have to get rid of that extra energy. they transfer this energy as heat to their neighboring atoms and molecules. In physics, this surrounding environment is historically called the “lattice”. As individual spins “fold” back up into the parallel state, the vertical magnetisation vector (\(M_z\)) starts to grow back from zero (\(M_z > 0\)). \[M_z = M_0 \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{T1}\right) \right]\]

\(M_0\) is the maximum possible magnetisation.
\(T1\) is defined as the exact amount of time it takes for the longitudinal magnetization to recover to 63% of its final maximum value (\(M_0\)). Different tissues in the body (like fat, muscle, and water) have completely different molecular “lattices.” Because of this, they transfer heat at different speeds, meaning they have different T1 time constants.

Now let’s focus on T2 Relaxation (Spin-Spin Relaxation). While T1 is about the spins returning to the main magnetic field (up/down), T2 is about the spins losing their synchronized dance (wobbling together). So while RF pulse all the proton spins are pointing in the exact same direction in the transverse (\(x,y\)) plane. They have “Max Phase coherence,”. The moment the RF pulse stops forcing them together, the protons begin to interact with the tiny magnetic fields of their immediate neighbors (spin-spin interactions). Because of these microscopic, “local inhomogeneities” in the magnetic field, some protons start to spin slightly faster and others slightly slower. => the perfectly aligned bundle of arrows begins to fan out. This “Loss of phase coherence” causes the measurable transverse magnetisation (\(M_{xy}\)) to drop to zero. \[M_{xy} = M_0 \exp\left(-\frac{t}{T2}\right)\]

\(M_0\) is the maximum possible transverse magnetisation (which occurs right after the RF pulse).
\(T2\) is defined as the time it takes for the transverse magnetisation to decay to about 37% of its initial value. Different tissues have different T2 times because the local magnetic field variations differ depending on the tissue’s molecular structure.

But it has a problem! Static Error.: the signal actually decays much, much faster than the natural T2 tissue limit predicts. The giant \(B_0\) magnet of the MRI machine is never perfectly uniform. These physical imperfections in the machine’s magnetic field force the protons out of phase almost instantly. Well remember Larmor frequency: \(\omega_0 = \gamma B_0\)? yeah some get stronger or weaker, so some spin faster or slower. Now we raise a new question: “How can the signal be restored at this point?”

title: X, Us, CT, MRI
section: Spin Echo to erase Static Error
text:
Spin Echo to erase Static Error
The goal is to rewind the fast decayed transverse magnetisation \(M_{xy}\) to 0. We do this by applying a 180° RF pulse at time \(\tau\) after the initial 90° pulse. This pulse - since is longer enough to flip the spins 180 degrees - effectively flips the spins, you only reverse the direction, so it will take EXACTLY the same time to COME BACK ALL TOGETHER FOR EVERY PROTON NO MATTER WHAT SPPED IT IS; BECAUSE IT IS SAME TIME TO GO BACK. At \(2\tau\), the spins rephase, and the transverse magnetization \(M_{xy}\) recovers to the second peak that is smaller.
Basically we will capture the signal at \(TE = 2\tau\), which touches the theoretical \(T_2\) decay curve (represents ONLY the natural decay caused by spin-spin interactions).
Basically all what we do is just to erase the static error (things that supposed to be equal will be back to equal). But the spin-spin error still exists (things not supposed to be equal will not anyway not equal anymore), so that is why the signal at \(TE = 2\tau\) is smaller than the signal at \(TE = 0\).
Understanding Mz under the 180 degree pulse (not so important just FYI):

At Time 0: The 90° pulse wipes out \(M_z\). It drops to exactly 0.
From 0 to \(TE/2\): The protons start giving off heat to their lattice (\(T_1\) relaxation). \(M_z\) starts slowly growing back up the positive Z-axis.
The 180° Flip (The Twist!): At \(TE/2\), the scanner fires the 180° pulse. This pulse flips everything. The \(M_z\) vector that just spent time growing upward is suddenly flipped upside down, pointing down the negative Z-axis!
From \(TE/2\) to \(TE\): \(M_z\) has to start recovering all over again, growing from the negative Z-axis, crossing through zero, and heading back up toward the positive Z-axis.

title: X, Us, CT, MRI
text:
From Signal to Image
If a tumor gives off a bright signal, the machine hears it, but it has no idea if that tumor is in the brain, the shoulder, or the knee.

Spatial Encoding
Let’s utilize Larmor equation again: \(\omega = \gamma * B_0\). so we can make a gradient magnetic field along the Z-axis (the main magnetic field direction).

Slice Selection Gradient (z-direction)
First, we apply a gradient from head-to-toe. We then fire an RF pulse that only matches the exact frequency of one single “slice” of the body. Protons outside this slice ignore the pulse, so we only get a signal from that one specific slice. The RF pulse physically washes over the entire body, but we set its frequency so that only one specific slice can “hear” it and react.
But in general we still do them one by one (sequentially). If the doctor wants 30 slices through your abdomen:

The machine turns on the Z-gradient.
It fires a 64.0 MHz pulse. (Slice 1 flips).
The machine turns off the pulse and listens for the echo from Slice 1.
It fires a 64.1 MHz pulse. (Slice 2 flips).
It listens for the echo from Slice 2.
It fires a 64.2 MHz pulse… and so on.




Procedure for y and x BASICALLY TWISTING IN BOTH DIRECTIONS to get K-SPACE

1. The Timeline of ONE Repetition (\(TR\))
Think of this as one single “tick” of the MRI clock. To get just one line of data for our raw data grid (k-space), the scanner executes these steps in this exact chronological order:

Step 1: Isolate the Slice (Z-Axis). The machine turns on the head-to-toe Z-gradient (\(G_z\)) and fires the 90° RF pulse. Result: Only the protons in one 2D slice of the brain are flipped.
Step 2: Twist the Phase (Y-Axis). The machine briefly turns on the top-to-bottom Y-gradient (\(G_y\)) and then turns it off. Result: The rows of protons are temporarily sped up and slowed down, giving them a permanent “phase twist.”
Step 3: Wait for the Echo (\(TE\)). The machine waits for the Spin Echo to physically rebuild itself.
Step 4: Read the Frequency (X-Axis). Exactly as the Spin Echo peaks, the machine turns on the left-to-right X-gradient (\(G_x\)) and turns on the microphone. Result: While the microphone is recording, the left side of the head is singing a low pitch, and the right side is singing a high pitch.Once Step 4 is done, the machine takes that one recording (which contains the X-frequencies and the Y-phase twist) and saves it as Row 1 of k-space.



2. 256-Step Loop
One repetition only gives us a 1D shadow. To get a 2D image, the architecture of an MRI scan is essentially a massive “For Loop” in a computer program.The scanner must repeat the 4-step timeline above 256 separate times. The only thing that changes in the entire timeline is the strength of Step 2 (The Phase Twist).

Loop 1: Run Steps 1-4 using a maximum positive phase twist. Save the echo to k-space Row 1. Wait for the protons to recover (\(TR\)).
Loop 2: Run Steps 1-4 using a slightly weaker phase twist. Save the echo to k-space Row 2. Wait for \(TR\).
Loop 3: Run Steps 1-4 using an even weaker phase twist. Save to Row 3. Wait for \(TR\)….
Loop 128: Run Steps 1-4 with zero phase twist. Save to Row 128 (the exact center of k-space). Wait for \(TR\)….
Loop 256: Run Steps 1-4 with a maximum negative phase twist. Save to Row 256.




Converting k-space to Image Space

k-Space
\[S(k_x, k_y) = A \cdot e^{i\phi}\]

\(A\): Amplitude (Signal Strength)
\(\phi\): Phase (Position Information)

where: \[S(k_x, k_y) = \iint \rho(x,y) \cdot e^{-i 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \,dx \,dy\]


Convert back to Image Space
\[I(x, y) = \iint S(k_x, k_y) e^{i 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \,dk_x \,dk_y\]

\(I(x, y)\): This is your final answer. It is the calculated Image Intensity (pixel brightness) at a specific physical physical coordinate (\(x, y\)) on the doctor’s screen.
\(S(k_x, k_y)\): This is the raw, complex radio signal stored at a specific coordinate (\(k_x, k_y\)) inside the k-space grid.

that means: \[(A \cdot e^{i\phi}) \times e^{i 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} = A \cdot e^{i(\phi + 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y))}\]

title: From Autoencoder to Dreamer
text:
Key Idea: Instead of mapping an input to a single point in the latent space, VAE maps it to parameters of a probability distribution (typically a Gaussian distribution) in the latent space:

An encoder that maps data to a distribution in latent space.
A decoder that maps samples from this latent distribution back to data space.



Let’s denote:

\(\mathbf{x}\): An input data point (e.g., an image)
\(\mathbf{z}\): A latent variable (vector) in the lower-dimensional latent space
\(p(\mathbf{x})\): The true, unknown data distribution we want to model
\(p(\mathbf{z})\): The prior distribution over the latent variables (typically a simple distribution like \(\mathcal{N}(0,I)\))
\(p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})\): The decoder (also called generative model or likelihood). This is a neural network parameterized by \(\theta\) that outputs the parameters of the distribution over \(\mathbf{x}\) given \(\mathbf{z}\).
\(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})\): The encoder (also called inference model or approximate posterior). This is a neural network parameterized by \(\phi\) that outputs the parameters of the distribution over \(\mathbf{z}\) given \(\mathbf{x}\).

Goal: is to maximize the marginal likelihood: \[p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})}\] which we do not know the other element to compute, or \[\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \log \int p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z}) d\mathbf{z}\] which is intractable
Therefore, VAEs optimize the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), which is a lower bound on the log-likelihood of the data, we will derive it now


The log-likelihood of a data point \(\mathbf{x}\) can be written as:
\[\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \log p_\theta(\mathbf{x})\] \[= \log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) \int q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{z} \quad \text{Multiply by 1}\] \[= \int q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) \log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{z} \quad \text{Bring inside the integral}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} [\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x})] \quad \text{Definition of expectation}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{p_\theta(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] \quad \text{Apply the equation } p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{p_\theta(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})}{p_\theta(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] \quad \text{Multiply by 1}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} + \log \frac{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})}{p_\theta(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] \quad \text{Split the expectation}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] + D_{KL}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) || p_\theta(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})) \quad \text{KL divergence}\]
The second term is the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, it is non-negative, now we only focus on maximizing the first term ELBO:
\[\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) \ge \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] \quad \text{Chain rule of probability}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} [\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})] + \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p(\mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] \quad \text{Split the expectation}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} [\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) || p(\mathbf{z})) \quad \text{KL divergence}\]
This is the objective function for the VAE (Loss Function), also the ELBO to maximize, or minimizing the negative ELBO.
\[\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi, \mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} [\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) || p(\mathbf{z}))\]



This term encourages the decoder to reconstruct the input \(\mathbf{x}\) accurately from a latent sample \(\mathbf{z}\) drawn from the encoder’s output distribution.
We will derive it now


We assume that each data point \(\mathbf{x}\) (e.g., an image, where each pixel is a dimension) is drawn from a Gaussian distribution, given the latent variable \(\mathbf{z}\) that the decoder outputs. For simplicity, let’s assume the dimensions of \(\mathbf{x}\) are independent given \(\mathbf{z}\), and they share a fixed variance \(\sigma^2\).
So, for each dimension \(j\) of \(\mathbf{x}\), \(x_j\) is distributed as: \(p_\theta(x_j|\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(x_j; \mu_j(\mathbf{z}), \sigma^2)\)
Here:

\(\mu_j(\mathbf{z})\) is the mean for the \(j\)-th dimension, which is the output of your decoder network for that dimension when given \(\mathbf{z}\).
\(\sigma^2\) is the variance. For simplicity, we often assume a fixed \(\sigma^2\) (e.g., \(\sigma^2=1\), or a small constant, or even absorbed into the weights). More advanced VAEs can learn this variance as well (the decoder outputs both mean and variance).




The PDF for a single-dimensional Gaussian variable \(x_j\) is:
\(f(x_j) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\)



Since we assume the dimensions of \(\mathbf{x}\) are independent given \(\mathbf{z}\), the joint probability \(p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})\) is the product of the individual probabilities:
\(p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}) = \prod_{j=1}^{D_x} p_\theta(x_j|\mathbf{z})\)
where \(D_x\) is the dimensionality of \(\mathbf{x}\) (e.g., number of pixels in an image). Now, let’s take the logarithm of this product:
\[\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}) = \log \left( \prod_{j=1}^{D_x} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \right)\] \[= \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} \log \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \right)\] \[= \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} \left( \log\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\right) + \log\left(\exp\left(-\frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\right) \right)\] \[= \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} \left( -\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)\] \[= \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} \left( -\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) \right) - \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} \left( \frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)\]The first term is a constant multiplied by \(D_x\): \[= -\frac{D_x}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} (x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2\] \[\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{D_x} \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} (x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2\]




This term acts as a regularizer. It pushes the approximate posterior \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})\) (the distribution output by the encoder for a given \(\mathbf{x}\)) to be close to the prior distribution \(p(\mathbf{z})\) (e.g., \(\mathcal{N}(0,I)\)).
If \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})\) is assumed to be a diagonal Gaussian \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)\) (where \(\Sigma\) is diagonal) and \(p(\mathbf{z})\) is \(\mathcal{N}(0,I)\), the KL divergence has a closed-form solution:
\[D_{KL}(\mathcal{N}(\mu,\Sigma)||\mathcal{N}(0,I)) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{D_z} (\exp(\sigma_j) + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \sigma_j)\] where \(D_z\) is the dimensionality of \(\mathbf{z}\), and \(\mu_j\) and \(\sigma_j\) are the mean and log-variance (diagonal elements of \(\Sigma\)) for the \(j\)-th latent dimension, as output by the encoder.


Let’s say our latent space \(\mathbf{z}\) is 2-dimensional (\(D_z=2\)). Our input is a specific image \(\mathbf{x}_{cat}\) (an image of a cat).
The encoder network takes \(\mathbf{x}_{cat}\) as input. Its output layer (after processing through several hidden layers) has two sets of \(D_z=2\) nodes:

Mean Output Nodes: For \(\mu_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{cat})\)
Log-Variance Output Nodes: For \(\log(\sigma_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{cat})^2)\) (we use log-variance for numerical stability, as variance must be positive).

Suppose for this specific \(\mathbf{x}_{cat}\), the encoder outputs:
\(\mu_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{cat})=\begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ -1.2 \end{bmatrix}\)
\(\log(\sigma_\phi(\mathbf{x}_{cat})^2)=\begin{bmatrix} -0.2 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}\)
From the log-variances, we calculate the variances:
\(\sigma_1^2 = \exp(-0.2) \approx 0.8187\) \(\sigma_2^2 = \exp(0.1) \approx 1.1052\)
So, for this input \(\mathbf{x}_{cat}\), the encoder defines the latent distribution: \[q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_{cat})=\mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{z};\begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ -1.2 \end{bmatrix},\begin{bmatrix} 0.8187 & 0 \\ 0 & 1.1052 \end{bmatrix}\right)\]
This means:

The first latent dimension (\(z_1\)) is modeled by a Gaussian with mean 0.8 and variance 0.8187.
The second latent dimension (\(z_2\)) is modeled by a Gaussian with mean \(-1.2\) and variance 1.1052.

These two dimensions are assumed to be independent (because the off-diagonal elements of the covariance matrix are zero).
When we “sample \(\mathbf{z}\) from \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_{cat})\)” using the reparameterization trick, we would:

Sample \(\epsilon_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)\) and \(\epsilon_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)\).
Calculate \(z_1 = 0.8 + \sqrt{0.8187} \cdot \epsilon_1\)
Calculate \(z_2 = -1.2 + \sqrt{1.1052} \cdot \epsilon_2\)

The resulting \(\mathbf{z}=\begin{bmatrix} z_1 \\ z_2 \end{bmatrix}\) is then passed to the decoder.
This formulation allows the VAE to learn a distribution for each input, rather than a single point, enabling the desired properties of a continuous and meaningful latent space.



Imagine a very simplified VAE where our latent space \(\mathbf{z}\) is just one-dimensional (\(D_z=1\)). Our prior \(p(\mathbf{z})\) is \(\mathcal{N}(0,1)\) (mean 0, variance 1). The VAE’s job is to learn an encoder (\(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})\)) and a decoder (\(p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})\)) such that:

The decoder can reconstruct \(\mathbf{x}_A\) from samples of \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_A)\).
The decoder can reconstruct \(\mathbf{x}_B\) from samples of \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_B)\).
The KL divergence \(D_{KL}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})||p(\mathbf{z}))\) is minimized for both \(\mathbf{x}_A\) and \(\mathbf{x}_B\).

Scenario 1: No KL Regularization (like a vanilla Autoencoder)
If there were no KL term, the encoder might learn to map \(\mathbf{x}_A\) to a specific point \(\mathbf{z}_A\) and \(\mathbf{x}_B\) to a specific point \(\mathbf{z}_B\).

Encoder output for \(\mathbf{x}_A\): \(\mu_A=-5.0, \sigma_A=0.001\) (a very tight distribution at -5.0)
Encoder output for \(\mathbf{x}_B\): \(\mu_B=+5.0, \sigma_B=0.001\) (a very tight distribution at +5.0)

Scenario 2: With KL Regularization (VAE)
Now, the KL term \(D_{KL}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})||\mathcal{N}(0,1))\) is active.
Let’s say the encoder tries to map \(\mathbf{x}_A\) and \(\mathbf{x}_B\) far apart again:

Encoder output for \(\mathbf{x}_A\): \(\mu_A=-5.0, \sigma_A=0.1\)
Encoder output for \(\mathbf{x}_B\): \(\mu_B=+5.0, \sigma_B=0.1\)

Let’s calculate the KL divergence for \(\mathbf{x}_A\): \[D_{KL}(\mathcal{N}(-5.0,0.1^2)||\mathcal{N}(0,1)) = \frac{1}{2} (\exp(2\log(0.1))+(-5.0)^2-1-2\log(0.1))\] \[= \frac{1}{2} (0.01+25-1-(-4.6)) = \frac{1}{2} (24.01+4.6)=14.3\]
This KL value (14.3) is very high! The VAE loss function will strongly penalize this. To minimize this KL term, the encoder is forced to:

Pull the means towards 0: \(\mu_A\) and \(\mu_B\) must be closer to 0.
Push the variances towards 1: \(\sigma_A\) and \(\sigma_B\) must be closer to 1.

So, for similar inputs \(\mathbf{x}_A\) and \(\mathbf{x}_B\), the trained encoder might output:

Encoder output for \(\mathbf{x}_A\): \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_A)=\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z};\mu_A=-0.5,\sigma_A=0.8)\)
Encoder output for \(\mathbf{x}_B\): \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_B)=\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z};\mu_B=+0.5,\sigma_B=0.8)\)

Now, let’s evaluate the KL divergence again (for \(\mathbf{x}_A\)): \[D_{KL}(\mathcal{N}(-0.5,0.8^2)||\mathcal{N}(0,1)) = \frac{1}{2} (\exp(2\log(0.8))+(-0.5)^2-1-2\log(0.8))\] \[= \frac{1}{2} (0.64+0.25-1-(-0.446))= \frac{1}{2} (-0.11+0.446)=0.168\]
This KL value (0.168) is much smaller! The KL regularization successfully compressed the latent distributions closer to the prior.
Why this helps:
The only way for the encoder to reconstruct accurately AND stay close to the prior when presented with similar inputs is to map them to overlapping distributions.





Let’s consider two distinct but very similar input data points, \(\mathbf{x}_A\) and \(\mathbf{x}_B\), both from training distribution
Pressure from KL Divergence: Make them similar

For \(\mathbf{x}_A\), the encoder will produce \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_A)=\mathcal{N}(\mu_A,\Sigma_A)\). The KL term wants \(\mu_A \approx 0\) and \(\Sigma_A \approx I\).
For \(\mathbf{x}_B\), the encoder will produce \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_B)=\mathcal{N}(\mu_B,\Sigma_B)\). The KL term wants \(\mu_B \approx 0\) and \(\Sigma_B \approx I\).

The mathematical consequence of minimizing \(D_{KL}(Q||P)\) is that \(Q\) is forced to be similar to \(P\). Since \(P\) is the same prior for all \(\mathbf{x}\), this means all \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})\) distributions for any \(\mathbf{x}\) are pulled towards the same central region of the latent space (around 0). This inherently forces them to be closer to each other than they might be in a vanilla autoencoder without this regularization.
Pressure from Reconstruction Loss: Make them distinct
If the encoder were to map \(\mathbf{x}_A\) and \(\mathbf{x}_B\) to identical distributions (e.g., \(\mu_A=\mu_B=0\) and \(\Sigma_A=\Sigma_B=I\)), the reconstruction loss would be high to penalize that.
The Interplay (The “Dual Pressure”):

The KL term pushes all latent distributions for different \(\mathbf{x}\) towards the same central region of the latent space and encourages them to have a certain “spread” (variance \(\approx I\)). This means they will naturally overlap.
The reconstruction term pulls these distributions slightly apart (or adjusts their means and variances) just enough so that the decoder can distinguish between similar inputs and reconstruct them accurately.

The balance between these two forces is key. The optimal solution is where the encoder maps similar inputs \(\mathbf{x}_A\) and \(\mathbf{x}_B\) to latent distributions \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_A)\) and \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_B)\) that are:

Close to each other (due to KL regularization towards the common prior).
Significantly overlapping (due to the variances being pushed towards 1 by KL, and their means being close).
Slightly distinct in their means/variances such that the decoder can still reconstruct \(\mathbf{x}_A\) from samples of \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_A)\) and \(\mathbf{x}_B\) from samples of \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_B)\) with low reconstruction error.



A challenge arises because sampling \(\mathbf{z}\) from \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})\) is a stochastic operation, which makes backpropagation through the sampling step difficult. The reparameterization trick solves this. Instead of sampling \(\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\Sigma)\), we sample an auxiliary random variable \(\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)\) and then compute:
\[\mathbf{z} = \mu_\phi(\mathbf{x}) + \sigma_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}\] (where \(\odot\) is element-wise multiplication, and \(\sigma_\phi(\mathbf{x})\) is the standard deviation, often computed from log-variance to ensure positivity). Now, the stochasticity is moved to \(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}\), and \(\mathbf{z}\) becomes a deterministic function of \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), and \(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}\), allowing gradients to flow back through \(\mu_\phi(\mathbf{x})\) and \(\sigma_\phi(\mathbf{x})\) to the encoder’s parameters \(\phi\).





Generative Model: By sampling a \(\mathbf{z}\) from the simple prior \(p(\mathbf{z})\) (e.g., standard normal) and passing it through the decoder \(p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})\), we can generate entirely new data that resembles the training data
Variational Inference & Trade-off between Reconstruction and Regularization: The balance between the reconstruction loss and the KL divergence term is critical. If the KL term is too strong, the model might prioritize learning the prior over accurate reconstruction, leading to blurry reconstructions. If too weak, the latent space might not be well-regularized for generation. This balance is often controlled by a weighting factor (beta-VAE).

title: From Autoencoder to Dreamer
text:
Let’s denote:

\(\mathbf{x}\): An input data point (e.g., an image)
\(\mathbf{z}\): A latent variable (vector) in the lower-dimensional latent space
\(p(\mathbf{x})\): The true, unknown data distribution we want to model
\(p(\mathbf{z})\): The prior distribution over the latent variables (typically a simple distribution like \(\mathcal{N}(0,I)\))
\(p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})\): The decoder (also called generative model or likelihood). This is a neural network parameterized by \(\theta\) that outputs the parameters of the distribution over \(\mathbf{x}\) given \(\mathbf{z}\).
\(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})\): The encoder (also called inference model or approximate posterior). This is a neural network parameterized by \(\phi\) that outputs the parameters of the distribution over \(\mathbf{z}\) given \(\mathbf{x}\).

Goal: is to maximize the marginal likelihood: \[p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})}\] which we do not know the other element to compute, or \[\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \log \int p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z}) d\mathbf{z}\] which is intractable
Therefore, VAEs optimize the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), which is a lower bound on the log-likelihood of the data, we will derive it now


The log-likelihood of a data point \(\mathbf{x}\) can be written as:
\[\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \log p_\theta(\mathbf{x})\] \[= \log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) \int q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{z} \quad \text{Multiply by 1}\] \[= \int q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) \log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{z} \quad \text{Bring inside the integral}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} [\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x})] \quad \text{Definition of expectation}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{p_\theta(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] \quad \text{Apply the equation } p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{p_\theta(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})}{p_\theta(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] \quad \text{Multiply by 1}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} + \log \frac{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})}{p_\theta(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] \quad \text{Split the expectation}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] + D_{KL}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) || p_\theta(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})) \quad \text{KL divergence}\]
The second term is the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, it is non-negative, now we only focus on maximizing the first term ELBO:
\[\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}) \ge \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}) p(\mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] \quad \text{Chain rule of probability}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} [\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})] + \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p(\mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] \quad \text{Split the expectation}\] \[= \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} [\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) || p(\mathbf{z})) \quad \text{KL divergence}\]
This is the objective function for the VAE (Loss Function), also the ELBO to maximize, or minimizing the negative ELBO.
\[\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi, \mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} \right] = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})} [\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) || p(\mathbf{z}))\]



This term encourages the decoder to reconstruct the input \(\mathbf{x}\) accurately from a latent sample \(\mathbf{z}\) drawn from the encoder’s output distribution.
We will derive it now


We assume that each data point \(\mathbf{x}\) (e.g., an image, where each pixel is a dimension) is drawn from a Gaussian distribution, given the latent variable \(\mathbf{z}\) that the decoder outputs. For simplicity, let’s assume the dimensions of \(\mathbf{x}\) are independent given \(\mathbf{z}\), and they share a fixed variance \(\sigma^2\).
So, for each dimension \(j\) of \(\mathbf{x}\), \(x_j\) is distributed as: \(p_\theta(x_j|\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(x_j; \mu_j(\mathbf{z}), \sigma^2)\)
Here:

\(\mu_j(\mathbf{z})\) is the mean for the \(j\)-th dimension, which is the output of your decoder network for that dimension when given \(\mathbf{z}\).
\(\sigma^2\) is the variance. For simplicity, we often assume a fixed \(\sigma^2\) (e.g., \(\sigma^2=1\), or a small constant, or even absorbed into the weights). More advanced VAEs can learn this variance as well (the decoder outputs both mean and variance).




The PDF for a single-dimensional Gaussian variable \(x_j\) is:
\(f(x_j) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\)



Since we assume the dimensions of \(\mathbf{x}\) are independent given \(\mathbf{z}\), the joint probability \(p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})\) is the product of the individual probabilities:
\(p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}) = \prod_{j=1}^{D_x} p_\theta(x_j|\mathbf{z})\)
where \(D_x\) is the dimensionality of \(\mathbf{x}\) (e.g., number of pixels in an image). Now, let’s take the logarithm of this product:
\[\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}) = \log \left( \prod_{j=1}^{D_x} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \right)\] \[= \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} \log \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \right)\] \[= \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} \left( \log\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\right) + \log\left(\exp\left(-\frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\right) \right)\] \[= \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} \left( -\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)\] \[= \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} \left( -\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) \right) - \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} \left( \frac{(x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)\]The first term is a constant multiplied by \(D_x\): \[= -\frac{D_x}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} (x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2\] \[\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{D_x} \sum_{j=1}^{D_x} (x_j - \mu_j(\mathbf{z}))^2\]




This term acts as a regularizer. It pushes the approximate posterior \(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})\) (the distribution output by the encoder for a given \(\mathbf{x}\)) to be close to the prior distribution \(p(\mathbf{z})\) (e.g., \(\mathcal{N}(0,I)\)).
