The first way to solve PnP is the DLT method. Imagine the first pose is considered as the world frame. We want to find the camera pose \([\mathbf{R}|\mathbf{t}]\), which is a \(3 \times 4\) matrix.
Consider a 3D point \(P\) in the earlier camera frame (also now considered as world frame) with homogeneous coordinates:\[\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z, 1)^T\]
And its projection in the image as normalized coordinates:\[\mathbf{x}_1 = (u_1, v_1, 1)^T\]
Our goal now is to find the shift of that same point between the earlier camera frame and the current camera frame. OK, so let’s expand the transformation of the 3D point \(\mathbf{P}_w\) (in World coordinates) into the camera’s local frame \(\mathbf{P}_c\):\[\mathbf{P}_c = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{P}_w + \mathbf{t}\] Now, we project that local 3D point \(\mathbf{P}_c\) into pixels \(\mathbf{p}\) using the Intrinsic matrix \(\mathbf{K}\):\[s \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{K} \mathbf{P}_c\] \[s \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{K} (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{P}_w + \mathbf{t})\] In the DLT derivation, we combined everything into one matrix \(\mathbf{M}\):\[s \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{M} \mathbf{P}_w \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{M} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} | \mathbf{t}]\] When we solve for \(\mathbf{M}\) using those linear equations (DLT), we are solving for a matrix that contains both the lens info (\(\mathbf{K}\)) and the pose (\(\mathbf{R, t}\)).Since we already know \(\mathbf{K}\) (from calibration), once we find \(\mathbf{M}\), we can “extract” the pose:\[[\mathbf{R} | \mathbf{t}] = \mathbf{K}^{-1} \mathbf{M}\]
Let’s start at the projection relationship:\[s \begin{pmatrix} u_1 \\ v_1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} t_1 & t_2 & t_3 & t_4 \\ t_5 & t_6 & t_7 & t_8 \\ t_9 & t_{10} & t_{11} & t_{12} \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R}|\mathbf{t}]} \begin{pmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\]

WAIT, WELL uhm uhm, isnt this the Projection Matrix for Camera Calibration???? Right, in calibration we are just trying to find the whole parameters, but here we are just trying to find R|t, the shift of the observed points in current camera coordinates relative to the point location from origin coordinates. Because normally, you actually only need intrinsic K to know physical relationship between points in camera coords. Extrinsic is exactly the thing telling camera location in world coordinate, it is magically also what we are looking for

Just as we did with Homography, because the depth s exists in all rows, we use the third row of the matrix to eliminate the unknown depth \(s\). This gives us two linear constraints for every point:\[u_1 = \frac{t_1 X + t_2 Y + t_3 Z + t_4}{t_9 X + t_{10} Y + t_{11} Z + t_{12}}\]\[v_1 = \frac{t_5 X + t_6 Y + t_7 Z + t_8}{t_9 X + t_{10} Y + t_{11} Z + t_{12}}\]
To make the math cleaner, let’s define three row vectors from the \(3 \times 4\) transformation matrix \(\mathbf{T}\):\[\mathbf{t}_1 = (t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4)^T \quad \mathbf{t}_2 = (t_5, t_6, t_7, t_8)^T \quad \mathbf{t}_3 = (t_9, t_{10}, t_{11}, t_{12})^T\]
So basically we have: \[s \begin{pmatrix} u_1 \\ v_1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{t}_1^T \\ \mathbf{t}_2^T \\ \mathbf{t}_3^T \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{P}\]
Substitute \(s = \mathbf{t}_3^T \mathbf{P}\) into the first two equations:\[(\mathbf{t}_3^T \mathbf{P}) \cdot u_1 = \mathbf{t}_1^T \mathbf{P} \implies \mathbf{t}_1^T \mathbf{P} - u_1 \mathbf{t}_3^T \mathbf{P} = 0\]\[(\mathbf{t}_3^T \mathbf{P}) \cdot v_1 = \mathbf{t}_2^T \mathbf{P} \implies \mathbf{t}_2^T \mathbf{P} - v_1 \mathbf{t}_3^T \mathbf{P} = 0\]
If we have \(N\) points, we stack these into a giant matrix equation:\[\begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{P}_1^T & 0 & -u_1 \mathbf{P}_1^T \\ 0 & \mathbf{P}_1^T & -v_1 \mathbf{P}_1^T \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ \mathbf{P}_N^T & 0 & -u_N \mathbf{P}_N^T \\ 0 & \mathbf{P}_N^T & -v_N \mathbf{P}_N^T \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{t}_1 \\ \mathbf{t}_2 \\ \mathbf{t}_3 \end{pmatrix} = 0 \tag{6.27}\]
Solving: Just like the 8-point algorithm, we have 12 unknowns. Each point provides 2 equations, so we need at least 6 points to solve this directly. Or if we have more than 6 points, just like the Essential matrix in epipolar geometry, we can also find the least-square solution of the overdetermined equation.
The DLT treats all 12 elements of \(\mathbf{T}\) as independent. However, the first \(3 \times 3\) block of \(\mathbf{T}\) must be a valid Rotation Matrix (\(\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)\)). Because of noise, the DLT result is usually just a “general matrix” that might be slightly skewed or scaled. To “fix” it, we project the result back onto the \(SO(3)\) manifold (making it orthogonal with a determinant of 1). One way to approximate the “closest” valid rotation matrix is: \[\mathbf{R} \leftarrow (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{R}^T)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \mathbf{R} \tag{6.28}\]

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Stereo Camera
section: Dummy reminder for the depth s!
text:
Dummy reminder for the depth s!
If u ever wonder why we keep having the \(s\)-cale in the equation, Let’s start with the real pinhole camera model. Imagine a 3D point \(\mathbf{P} = (X, Y, Z)\) in the real world. Light travels from that point through the camera’s pinhole (the origin) and hits the image sensor.Because of similar triangles, the relationship between the 3D position and the 2D pixel \((u, v)\) is:\[u = f \frac{X}{Z}, \quad v = f \frac{Y}{Z}\] \[Z \cdot u = f \cdot X\] \[Z \cdot v = f \cdot Y\] So yeah as you see, we have: \[s \begin{pmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \mathbf{P}\] where \(s\) is the depth of the point in the camera frame.

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Stereo Camera
text:
Conceptually BA
The linear method mentioned above is often divided into many steps, estimating the camera pose first and then the point’s position, or the opposite. We can also construct the PnP problem as a nonlinear least-square problem about reprojection errors that treats them as optimization variables and optimizes them together.
Goal: We want to find the camera pose \(\mathbf{T}\) that makes our 3D map points “land” as close as possible to the pixels we actually see. For each 3D point \(\mathbf{P}_i\) and its observed pixel \(\mathbf{u}_i\): \[s_i \mathbf{u}_i = \mathbf{K}\mathbf{T}\mathbf{P}_i\]

\(\mathbf{u}_i = [u_i, v_i, 1]^T\): The observed pixel in the image.
\(\mathbf{T}\): The camera pose we want to find (containing \(\mathbf{R}\) and \(\mathbf{t}\)).
\(\mathbf{P}_i = [X_i, Y_i, Z_i, 1]^T\): The 3D point in the World Frame.
\(s_i\): The depth of the point.

We want to find the camera pose \(\mathbf{T}\) that minimizes the sum of all reprojection errors. Because of noise, the projection \(\frac{1}{s_i} \mathbf{K}\mathbf{T}\mathbf{P}_i\) will never perfectly equal the pixel \(\mathbf{u}_i\). We calculate the Residual (the error) and try to minimize the sum of all these errors: \[\mathbf{T}^* = \arg \min_{\mathbf{T}} \sum_{i} e_i^2\] \[\mathbf{T}^* = \arg \min_{\mathbf{T}} \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\| \mathbf{u}_i - \frac{1}{s_i} \mathbf{K}\mathbf{T}\mathbf{P}_i \right\|_2^2\]

The Calculation: Every single point \(i\) produces its own error (\(e_i\))—the distance between where it should be and where it is on the screen.
The Stacking: To solve the system, you stack all 100 errors into one giant vector \(\mathbf{e}\) and all 100 Jacobians into one giant matrix \(\mathbf{J}\).
The Goal: You are using the information from all points \(i\) to find one single update (\(\Delta \mathbf{T}\)) for the camera.

Since the projection function is non-linear (because of the division by depth \(s_i\) and the rotation matrix), we solve it iteratively using methods like Gauss-Newton or Levenberg-Marquardt. We linearize the error \(\mathbf{e}\) using the Jacobian matrix (\(\mathbf{J}\)): \[\mathbf{e}(\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x}) \approx \mathbf{e}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{J}^T \Delta\mathbf{x}\]

Conceptually: \(\mathbf{x}\) is the camera pose \(\mathbf{T}\).
Numerically, optimization algorithms like Gauss-Newton need to work with vectors so they can calculate “steps” (\(\Delta\mathbf{x}\)). Therefore, \(\mathbf{x}\) is the camera pose expressed in Lie Algebra (\(\mathfrak{se}(3)\)).

The State (\(\mathbf{x}\)): A 6-dimensional vector \(\xi = [\rho, \phi]^T\).
The Transformation: We convert it back to the matrix form using the exponential map: \(\mathbf{T} = \exp(\xi^\wedge)\).


Now, we substitute our linear approximation back into our cost function. We want to find the \(\Delta \mathbf{x}\) that makes the error as small as possible:\[E(\Delta \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2} \| \mathbf{e} + \mathbf{J} \Delta \mathbf{x} \|^2\] \[E(\Delta \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{e}^T\mathbf{e} + 2\mathbf{e}^T\mathbf{J}\Delta\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{J}\Delta\mathbf{x})\] \[\frac{d E}{d \Delta \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{e} + \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J} \Delta \mathbf{x} = 0\] \[\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J} \Delta \mathbf{x} = -\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{e}\]

\(\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J}\): This is often called the Hessian approximation (\(\mathbf{H}\)). It represents the “curvature” of our error surface. It tells the computer how “steep” the hill is in every direction.
\(-\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{e}\): This is the Negative Gradient (\(-\mathbf{g}\)). It points directly “downhill.”


“Take the downhill direction (\(-\mathbf{g}\)) and scale it by the local curvature (\(\mathbf{H}\)) to find exactly how big of a step (\(\Delta \mathbf{x}\)) I should take to reach the bottom.”


Initialize the camera pose \(\mathbf{T}_0\).
For \(k = 0, 1, 2, \dots\):

Calculate the reprojection error \(e_i\) for each point.
Calculate the Jacobian matrix \(\mathbf{J}_i\) of the reprojection error with respect to the camera pose.
Solve the linear system \(\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J} \Delta \mathbf{T} = -\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{e}\) for the update step \(\Delta \mathbf{T}\).
Update the camera pose \(\mathbf{T}_{k+1} = \mathbf{T}_k + \Delta \mathbf{T}\).

Repeat until convergence.

Here, \(\Delta \mathbf{T}\) is a 6-dimensional Lie Algebra vector (\(\delta \xi\)). The “update” in Step 2d actually looks like this in the code:\[\mathbf{T}_{k+1} = \exp(\delta \xi^\wedge) \mathbf{T}_k\].

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Stereo Camera
text:
Formulation
Since the camera pose lives on a manifold (\(SE(3)\)), we can’t take a “normal” derivative. Instead, we use the perturbation model we learned in the Lie Algebra blog I have written. We apply a tiny “nudge” \(\delta\xi\) and see how much the pixel moves.
First, we take a 3D point \(\mathbf{P}\) from the world and transform it into the local coordinates of the current camera. We call this local 3D point \(\mathbf{P}'\):\[\mathbf{P}' = (\mathbf{TP})_{1:3} = [X', Y', Z']^T\]
Now, we project that local 3D point \(\mathbf{P}'\) onto the image sensor using the Intrinsic matrix \(\mathbf{K}\):\[s\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{K}\mathbf{P}'\]
\[\begin{bmatrix} su \\ sv \\ s \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X' \\ Y' \\ Z' \end{bmatrix}\]
To get the actual pixel coordinates (\(u, v\)), we divide by the depth (\(s = Z'\)) using the third row: \[u = f_x \frac{X'}{Z'} + c_x, \quad v = f_y \frac{Y'}{Z'} + c_y\]
The Jacobian \(\mathbf{J}\) represents how the error \(\mathbf{e}\) (the distance between projected and observed pixels) changes when we wiggle the camera pose by a tiny amount \(\delta\xi\).Using the Chain Rule, we break this derivative into two manageable pieces:\[\frac{\partial \mathbf{e}}{\partial \delta \xi} = \lim_{\delta \xi \to 0} \frac{\mathbf{e}(\delta \xi \oplus \xi) - \mathbf{e}(\xi)}{\delta \xi} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{e}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'} \frac{\partial \mathbf{P}'}{\partial \delta \xi}\]

\(\frac{\partial \mathbf{e}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'}\) (Dimension \(2 \times 3\)): This is the Projection Jacobian. It asks: “If I move the 3D point \(P'\) slightly in camera space, how much do the pixels \(u, v\) move?” (This depends only on the lens/intrinsics).
\(\frac{\partial \mathbf{P}'}{\partial \delta \xi}\) (Dimension \(3 \times 6\)): This is the Pose Jacobian. It asks: “If I wiggle the camera pose \(\delta\xi\), how much does the 3D point \(P'\) appear to move relative to the camera?” (This depends on Lie Algebra).



The Goal: Find the \(2 \times 6\) matrix that relates pose-wiggles to pixel-wiggles.
The Strategy: Use \(\mathbf{P}'\) as a “middle-man.”
The “\(\oplus\)” symbol: This represents left-multiplication in \(SE(3)\). Instead of adding a vector, we are multiplying the current pose by a tiny incremental rotation and translation.

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Stereo Camera
section: Solving to Update Camera Pose (Visual Odometry)
text:
Solving to Update Camera Pose (Visual Odometry)
We start by finding how the pixel error \(\mathbf{e}\) changes with respect to the 3D point position in the camera frame \(\mathbf{P}' = [X', Y', Z']^T\). By differentiating the projection equations from the pinhole camera model from previous chapter, we get this \(2 \times 3\) matrix:\[\frac{\partial \mathbf{e}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'} = - \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial u}{\partial X'} & \frac{\partial u}{\partial Y'} & \frac{\partial u}{\partial Z'} \\ \frac{\partial v}{\partial X'} & \frac{\partial v}{\partial Y'} & \frac{\partial v}{\partial Z'} \end{bmatrix} = - \begin{bmatrix} \frac{f_x}{Z'} & 0 & -\frac{f_x X'}{Z'^2} \\ 0 & \frac{f_y}{Z'} & -\frac{f_y Y'}{Z'^2} \end{bmatrix}\]
Next, we need to know how the 3D point \(\mathbf{P}'\) moves when we perturb the camera pose \(\delta \xi\). Using the Lie algebra perturbation model (this will be introduced in another blog):\[\frac{\partial (\mathbf{T P})}{\partial \delta \xi} = (\mathbf{T P})^\odot = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I} & -\mathbf{P}'^\wedge \\ \mathbf{0}^T & \mathbf{0}^T \end{bmatrix}\]
Since we only care about the 3D coordinates (the first 3 dimensions), we use:\[\frac{\partial \mathbf{P}'}{\partial \delta \xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I}, -\mathbf{P}'^\wedge \end{bmatrix}\]
When we multiply the two terms together (\(\frac{\partial \mathbf{e}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{P}'}{\partial \delta \xi}\)), we get the full Jacobian matrix for the camera pose:\[\frac{\partial \mathbf{e}}{\partial \delta \xi} = - \begin{bmatrix} \frac{f_x}{Z'} & 0 & -\frac{f_x X'}{Z'^2} & -\frac{f_x X' Y'}{Z'^2} & f_x + \frac{f_x X'^2}{Z'^2} & -\frac{f_x Y'}{Z'} \\ 0 & \frac{f_y}{Z'} & -\frac{f_y Y'}{Z'^2} & -f_y - \frac{f_y Y'^2}{Z'^2} & \frac{f_y X' Y'}{Z'^2} & \frac{f_y X'}{Z'} \end{bmatrix}\]
The first 3 columns correspond to translation (moving the camera), and the last 3 columns correspond to rotation (tilting the camera).

title: Feature-based Visual Odometry on Stereo Camera
section: Solving to Update Map Points (Map - BA)
text:
Solving to Update Map Points (Map - BA)
On top of optimizing the pose, we want to optimize the spatial position of the feature points. Therefore, we also need to discuss the derivative of \(\mathbf{e}\) with respect to the space point \(\mathbf{P}\). \[\frac{\partial \mathbf{e}}{\partial \mathbf{P}} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{e}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'} \frac{\partial \mathbf{P}'}{\partial \mathbf{P}}\]
Since \(\mathbf{P}' = \mathbf{R P} + \mathbf{t}\), the derivative \(\frac{\partial \mathbf{P}'}{\partial \mathbf{P}}\) is simply the rotation matrix \(\mathbf{R}\) we derived above. Plugging in our previous results: \[\frac{\partial \mathbf{e}}{\partial \mathbf{P}} = - \begin{bmatrix} \frac{f_x}{Z'} & 0 & -\frac{f_x X'}{Z'^2} \\ 0 & \frac{f_y}{Z'} & -\frac{f_y Y'}{Z'^2} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}\]
So yeah, combining these two, that’s how SLAM can simultaneously refine where the robot is and where the landmarks are.

title: Rendering
text:
\[L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) = L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) + \iint_{\Omega^{\text{in}}} \rho(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}, \omega^{\text{out}}) L_{\lambda}^{\text{in}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) \cos\theta^{\text{in}} d\Omega^{\text{in}}\] whereby:

\(\cos\theta^{\text{in}}\): Lambertian cosine term. \(\theta^{\text{in}}\) is the angle between the incoming direction \(\omega^{\text{in}}\) and the surface normal \(\mathbf{n}\). The more senkrecht, the more energy reflected!

Official rendering equation: \[L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) = L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) + \iint_{\Omega^{\text{in}}} \rho(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}, \omega^{\text{out}}) \mathbf{L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}(\text{trace}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}), -\omega^{\text{in}})} \cos\theta^{\text{in}} d\Omega^{\text{in}}\]
And you know what is important? We have proved this: \[d\Omega^{\text{in}} = \sin\theta^{\text{in}} d\theta^{\text{in}} d\phi\]
Area-Based Rendering Equation: \[\Delta\Omega_x = \frac{\cos\theta^{\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}}}{||\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y}||^2} \Delta A_y\] \[L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) = L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) + \iint_{\mathbf{y} \in A} \rho(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}}, \omega^{\text{out}}) L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}(\mathbf{y}, \omega^{\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}}) \mathbf{V(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})} \frac{\cos\theta^{\mathbf{x} \to \mathbf{y}} \cos\theta^{\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}}}{||\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y}||^2} d A_y\]
Full Geometry Term: \[G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{V(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})}\frac{\cos\theta^{\mathbf{x} \to \mathbf{y}} \cos\theta^{\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}}}{||\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y}||^2}\]



\[L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}} = L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}} + \mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{T} \cdot L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}\]

\(L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}\): The total outgoing radiance (the unknown we want to solve for).
\(L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}}\): The emitted light (the light sources).
\(\mathbf{T}: L_{\lambda}^{\text{in}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega) = L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}(\text{trace}(\mathbf{x}, \omega), -\omega)\)
\(\mathbf{R}: L_{\lambda}^{\text{reflect}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) = \iint_{\Omega^{\text{in}}} \rho(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}, \omega^{\text{out}}) L_{\lambda}^{\text{in}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) \cos\theta^{\text{in}} d\Omega^{\text{in}}\)
\(\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{T}\): The combined light transport operator. It takes light that is already outgoing from a surface (\(L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}\)), transports it to the next surface (\(T \cdot L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}} = L_{\lambda}^{\text{in}}\)), and reflects it (\(R \cdot L_{\lambda}^{\text{in}} = L_{\lambda}^{\text{reflect}}\)).

But how to solve this equation? \[L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{T})^{-1} L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}}\] Using Taylor series expansion: \[L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}} = L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}} + \mathbf{R} \mathbf{T} \cdot L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}} + (\mathbf{R} \mathbf{T})^2 \cdot L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}} + (\mathbf{R} \mathbf{T})^3 \cdot L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}} + \dots\]
where \(\mathbf{R} \mathbf{T}\) means reflected once, \((\mathbf{R} \mathbf{T})^2\) means reflected twice, etc.





Idea: All surfaces are diffuse => from Integral Equation (solved by Monte Carlo) to just a Linear Equation \(Ax=b\) solved by matrix inversion.
\[E_{\lambda, i}^{\text{out}} = E_{\lambda, i}^{\text{emit}} + \sum_{j} \rho_i F_{i \to j} E_{\lambda, j}^{\text{out}}\] where:

\(\rho_i\): The albedo of surface \(i\).
\(F_{i \to j}\): The form factor (geometric term) from surface \(i\) to surface \(j\).



Imagine 4 patches forming a small square “room”:

Patch 1 (Top): A light source. \(E_{1}^{\text{emit}} = 100\), \(\rho_1 = 0\) (absorbing).
Patch 2 (Bottom): A reflective floor. \(E_{2}^{\text{emit}} = 0\), \(\rho_2 = 0.8\).
Patch 3 (Left): A wall. \(E_{3}^{\text{emit}} = 0\), \(\rho_3 = 0.5\).
Patch 4 (Right): A wall. \(E_{4}^{\text{emit}} = 0\), \(\rho_4 = 0.5\).

The Form Factors (\(F_{i \to j}\)) Assume the geometry is such that patches see each other with the following visibility:

Opposite patches (1 to 2, 3 to 4) have \(F = 0.6\).
Adjacent patches (1 to 3, 1 to 4, etc.) have \(F = 0.2\).
Patches cannot see themselves, so \(F_{ii} = 0\).

Step 1: Set up the Equations The radiosity equation for each patch is: \(E_i = E_i^{\text{emit}} + \rho_i \sum (F_{ij} E_j)\).

\(E_1\): \(100 + 0 = \mathbf{100}\) (Since \(\rho_1 = 0\), it only emits).
\(E_2\): \(0 + 0.8 \cdot (0.6 E_1 + 0.2 E_3 + 0.2 E_4)\)
\(E_3\): \(0 + 0.5 \cdot (0.2 E_1 + 0.2 E_2 + 0.6 E_4)\)
\(E_4\): \(0 + 0.5 \cdot (0.2 E_1 + 0.2 E_2 + 0.6 E_3)\)

Step 2: Solve Iteratively (Gathering) Because these equations are interdependent (e.g., \(E_3\) depends on \(E_4\), and \(E_4\) depends on \(E_3\)), we solve them in “bounces”.

Iteration 1 (Direct Light only)

We plug \(E_1 = 100\) into the others, assuming \(E_{2,3,4} = 0\) initially:

\(E_2\): \(0.8 \cdot (0.6 \cdot 100) = \mathbf{48.0}\)
\(E_3\): \(0.5 \cdot (0.2 \cdot 100) = \mathbf{10.0}\)
\(E_4\): \(0.5 \cdot (0.2 \cdot 100) = \mathbf{10.0}\)
Iteration 2 (First Reflection/Bounce)

Now we plug the values from Iteration 1 back into the equations to see how light bounces between the walls:

\(E_2\): \(0.8 \cdot (60 + 0.2 \cdot 10 + 0.2 \cdot 10) = 0.8 \cdot (64) = \mathbf{51.2}\)
\(E_3\): \(0.5 \cdot (20 + 0.2 \cdot 48 + 0.6 \cdot 10) = 0.5 \cdot (20 + 9.6 + 6) = \mathbf{17.8}\)
\(E_4\): \(0.5 \cdot (20 + 0.2 \cdot 48 + 0.6 \cdot 10) = 0.5 \cdot (20 + 9.6 + 6) = \mathbf{17.8}\)

Step 3: Final Results If you continue this for 4–5 iterations, the numbers will stop changing (converge).

Patch 1: 100 (The Sun)
Patch 2: \(\approx 54\) (Bright floor)
Patch 3 & 4: \(\approx 22\) (Dimmer walls)




Imagine Patch 2 is a mirror. Now, instead of one value for \(E_2\), you need an infinite number of values for every possible direction \(\omega\). So for every point, we have this directional-based integral again: \[L_o(x, \omega_o) = L_e(x, \omega_o) + \int_{\Omega} \rho(x, \omega_i, \omega_o) L_i(x, \omega_i) \cos \theta_i d\omega_i\]
So what change? For example:

Diffuse Rule: Light from the Bulb (1) hits the Floor (2) and splits 20% to Wall 3 and 20% to Wall 4.
Glossy Rule: Light from the Bulb (1) hits the Floor (2). Because of the angle, 90% of that reflected light is directed at Wall 4, and 0% goes to Wall 3.

So we dont have that 20% \(\rho\) for every direction anymore, we have \(\rho(x, \omega_i, \omega_o)\) always varying. We have to use Monte Carlo to sample the direction.




In Path Tracing, we start with \(N=1\) at the pixel, \(N=1\) at the first bounce, \(N=1\) at the second bounce, and so on, creates a single, complete light path. yeah basically \(N=1\) everywhere. So yes, only \(N=1\) sample per integration step.


When \(N=1\), the average \(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N\) becomes just a single term:\[F = \int f(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} \approx \hat{f}_1 = \frac{f(\mathbf{x}_0)}{p(\mathbf{x}_0)} \quad \text{}\]

\(\mathbf{x}_0\): A single, randomly chosen light path direction
\(\hat{f}_1\): The result of this single, weighted sample




Averaging at the Pixel Level: THIS IS IMPORTANT!!! The averaging (\(\frac{1}{N}\)) is moved outside the recursion. To get the final image, the renderer traces millions of these \(N=1\) light paths (samples) for each pixel and averages the results together. This overall average still converges to the correct answer \(\bar{f}\), as proven by the Central Limit Theorem.






We want to build this path: \(y_0 \to \dots \to y_j \to x_i \to \dots \to x_1\). There are so many thing that we randomly sample:

Random point on light source
Random pixel
Random direction (BRDF)
Russian Roulette at each bounce

Each connection is a sample of the total light transport, so we Monte Carlo it: \[F \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{f(x_i)}{p(x_i)}\]



Using your 4-patch example (Light, Floor, Walls), imagine a path with 3 segments: Light \(\to\) Floor \(\to\) Wall \(\to\) Eye. This path can be generated by:

Tracing 3 steps from the light and 0 from the eye.
Tracing 2 steps from the light and 1 from the eye.
Tracing 1 step from the light and 2 from the eye.

Each of these is a different sampling strategy for the exact same physical path. If you just added them together, the image would be far too bright and physically incorrect.
MIS assigns a weight (\(w\)) to each strategy so that the total contribution equals 1. It follows the Balance Heuristic: \[w_i(P) = \frac{p_i(P)}{\sum_{j} p_j(P)}\]
\(p_i(P)\): The probability of generating path \(P\) using strategy \(i\).




Are probabilities for the Path or the Segment?

The probability \(p\) used in MIS is for the entire path. \[p(\text{Path}) = p(x_0) \cdot p(x_1|x_0) \cdot p(x_2|x_1) \dots\]

How are \(p_A\) and \(p_B\) calculated automatically?

To calculate the PDF for a strategy, the computer looks at the sampling rules used during the Monte Carlo process.

Strategy A (Camera-heavy): \(p_A\) = (Pixel PDF) \(\times\) (BRDF PDF) \(\times\) (Geometry Factor).
Strategy B (Light-heavy): \(p_B\) = (Light Area PDF) \(\times\) (Emission PDF) \(\times\) (BRDF Reflection PDF).

Once the computer has these two numbers (\(p_A\) and \(p_B\)), it uses the Balance Heuristic automatically: \[w_B = \frac{p_B}{p_A + p_B}\]
If \(p_B\) is 1000 and \(p_A\) is 0.001 (because hitting a tiny bulb is hard), then \(w_B\) becomes 0.9999.The computer doesn’t “know” it’s a glossy floor; it just sees that the math for Strategy B resulted in a much higher probability density for that specific path than Strategy A did.







Imagine exactly like a camera model. \(j\) is a pixel the image plane in the back. In front of the image plane is a hole/apeture of size \(A\), and \(x\) is a point on that hole/apeture.
The brightness (\(\Phi_{\lambda,j}\)) of a pixel \(j\): \[\Phi_{\lambda,j} = \iint_{x \in A} \iint_{\Omega_j^{in}(x)} W_\lambda^e(x, \omega^{in}) L_\lambda^{in}(x, \omega^{in}) \cos \theta^{in} d\Omega_j^{in} dA\]

It integrates all incoming radiance (\(L_\lambda^{in}\)) over each point \(x\) on the apeture \(A\), and each direction \(\omega^{in}\) that the light can come from. Some of the direction \(\omega^{in}\) going through point \(x\) will not point to pixel \(j\). But some will, we call this \(W_\lambda^e(x, \omega^{in})\) as follow. this part is the most confusing part for me until now.
If the ray coming from direction \(\omega^{\text{in}}\) at point \(\mathbf{x}\) hits the target pixel \(j\), then:\[W_{\lambda}^e(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) = 1\]
If the ray misses pixel \(j\) or the entire sensor, then:\[W_{\lambda}^e(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) = 0\]




Trace back a single race and see how much influence it has, 40% or 60%?… \[W_\lambda^{in}(x, \omega^{in}) = W_\lambda^e(x, \omega^{in}) + \iint_{\Omega^{out}} \rho(x, \omega^{out}, \omega^{in}) W_\lambda^{in}(trace(x, \omega^{out}), -\omega^{out}) \cos \theta^{out} d\Omega^{out}\]
CẨN THẬN ĐỌC NHẦM, TÚM LẠI LÀ TRACE IN OUT NGƯỢC LẠI SO VỚI RENDERING
How to use this? Let’s make a simplified example. We have two surfaces, A and B. We want to find the Incoming Importance (\(W^{\text{in}}\)) at a ray arriving at surface B from a light source.

Pixel (\(\mathbf{x}_0\)) has Sensor Efficiency \(W^e = W_{\lambda}^e(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) = 1.0\) (Max influence)
Surface A (\(\mathbf{x}_1\)) has BRDF \(\rho_{A} = 0.5\) (Reflects 50% of influence)
Surface B (\(\mathbf{x}_2\)) has BRDF \(\rho_{B} = 0.8\) (Reflects 80% of influence)

Now we start at the sensor: \[W_{\lambda}^{in}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) = W^{\text{in}}_{\text{Pixel}} = W_{\lambda}^e(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) + 0 = 1.0 + 0 = 1.0\]
That means influence leaving the pixel is \(W^{\text{out}}_{\text{Pixel}} = 1.0\).
Now we go to surface A. Surface A is the first surface hit by the ray traced backward from the pixel. Outgoing Importance \(W^{\text{out}}_{\text{A}}\) (Toward the Pixel): \[W^{\text{out}}_{\text{A} \to \text{Pixel}} = W^{\text{in}}_{\text{Pixel}} = 1.0\]
That means Influence leaving A toward the pixel = Influence arriving at the pixel from A.
Now we see what is the incoming importance at A- \(W^{\text{in}}_{\text{A}}\): \[W^{\text{in}}_{\text{A}} \approx \rho_{A} \cdot W^{\text{out}}_{\text{A} \to \text{Pixel}}\] \[W^{\text{in}}_{\text{A}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.0 = 0.5\] That means a photon arriving at A only has \(50\%\) of the initial pixel’s influence, because A is a \(50\%\) reflector.
Surface B is hit after the ray scatters from A. Influence leaving B toward A = Influence arriving at A from B. \[W^{\text{out}}_{\text{B} \to \text{A}} = W^{\text{in}}_{\text{A}} = 0.5\]
Now Incoming Importance (\(W^{\text{in}}\)) at B. (which flows back to the light source): \[W^{\text{in}}_{\text{B}} \approx \rho_{B} \cdot W^{\text{out}}_{\text{B} \to \text{A}}\] \[W^{\text{in}}_{\text{B}} = 0.8 \cdot 0.5 = 0.4\]
Conclusion: The influence of \(1.0\) started at the pixel, traveled backward, lost \(50\%\) at surface A, and lost \(20\%\) at surface B, resulting in an overall accumulated influence of \(0.4\) for the entire path. The renderer now knows that if it finds a light path ending at B, that light must be weighted by \(0.4\) to contribute correctly to the pixel.

title: Rendering
text:
\[L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) = L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) + \iint_{\Omega^{\text{in}}} \rho(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}, \omega^{\text{out}}) L_{\lambda}^{\text{in}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) \cos\theta^{\text{in}} d\Omega^{\text{in}}\] whereby:

\(\cos\theta^{\text{in}}\): Lambertian cosine term. \(\theta^{\text{in}}\) is the angle between the incoming direction \(\omega^{\text{in}}\) and the surface normal \(\mathbf{n}\). The more senkrecht, the more energy reflected!

Official rendering equation: \[L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) = L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) + \iint_{\Omega^{\text{in}}} \rho(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}, \omega^{\text{out}}) \mathbf{L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}}(\text{trace}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}), -\omega^{\text{in}})} \cos\theta^{\text{in}} d\Omega^{\text{in}}\]
And you know what is important? We have proved this: \[d\Omega^{\text{in}} = \sin\theta^{\text{in}} d\theta^{\text{in}} d\phi\]

Full Geometry Term: \[G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{V(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})}\frac{\cos\theta^{\mathbf{x} \to \mathbf{y}} \cos\theta^{\mathbf{y} \to \mathbf{x}}}{||\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y}||^2}\]

Some of the direction \(\omega^{in}\) going through point \(x\) will not point to pixel \(j\). But some will, we call this \(W_\lambda^e(x, \omega^{in})\) as follow. this part is the most confusing part for me until now.
If the ray coming from direction \(\omega^{\text{in}}\) at point \(\mathbf{x}\) hits the target pixel \(j\), then:\[W_{\lambda}^e(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) = 1\]
If the ray misses pixel \(j\) or the entire sensor, then:\[W_{\lambda}^e(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) = 0\]




Trace back a single race and see how much influence it has, 40% or 60%?… \[W_\lambda^{in}(x, \omega^{in}) = W_\lambda^e(x, \omega^{in}) + \iint_{\Omega^{out}} \rho(x, \omega^{out}, \omega^{in}) W_\lambda^{in}(trace(x, \omega^{out}), -\omega^{out}) \cos \theta^{out} d\Omega^{out}\]
CẨN THẬN ĐỌC NHẦM, TÚM LẠI LÀ TRACE IN OUT NGƯỢC LẠI SO VỚI RENDERING
How to use this? Let’s make a simplified example. We have two surfaces, A and B. We want to find the Incoming Importance (\(W^{\text{in}}\)) at a ray arriving at surface B from a light source.

Pixel (\(\mathbf{x}_0\)) has Sensor Efficiency \(W^e = W_{\lambda}^e(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) = 1.0\) (Max influence)
Surface A (\(\mathbf{x}_1\)) has BRDF \(\rho_{A} = 0.5\) (Reflects 50% of influence)
Surface B (\(\mathbf{x}_2\)) has BRDF \(\rho_{B} = 0.8\) (Reflects 80% of influence)

Now we start at the sensor: \[W_{\lambda}^{in}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) = W^{\text{in}}_{\text{Pixel}} = W_{\lambda}^e(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{in}}) + 0 = 1.0 + 0 = 1.0\]
That means influence leaving the pixel is \(W^{\text{out}}_{\text{Pixel}} = 1.0\).
Now we go to surface A. Surface A is the first surface hit by the ray traced backward from the pixel. Outgoing Importance \(W^{\text{out}}_{\text{A}}\) (Toward the Pixel): \[W^{\text{out}}_{\text{A} \to \text{Pixel}} = W^{\text{in}}_{\text{Pixel}} = 1.0\]
That means Influence leaving A toward the pixel = Influence arriving at the pixel from A.
Now we see what is the incoming importance at A- \(W^{\text{in}}_{\text{A}}\): \[W^{\text{in}}_{\text{A}} \approx \rho_{A} \cdot W^{\text{out}}_{\text{A} \to \text{Pixel}}\] \[W^{\text{in}}_{\text{A}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.0 = 0.5\] That means a photon arriving at A only has \(50\%\) of the initial pixel’s influence, because A is a \(50\%\) reflector.
Surface B is hit after the ray scatters from A. Influence leaving B toward A = Influence arriving at A from B. \[W^{\text{out}}_{\text{B} \to \text{A}} = W^{\text{in}}_{\text{A}} = 0.5\]
Now Incoming Importance (\(W^{\text{in}}\)) at B. (which flows back to the light source): \[W^{\text{in}}_{\text{B}} \approx \rho_{B} \cdot W^{\text{out}}_{\text{B} \to \text{A}}\] \[W^{\text{in}}_{\text{B}} = 0.8 \cdot 0.5 = 0.4\]
Conclusion: The influence of \(1.0\) started at the pixel, traveled backward, lost \(50\%\) at surface A, and lost \(20\%\) at surface B, resulting in an overall accumulated influence of \(0.4\) for the entire path. The renderer now knows that if it finds a light path ending at B, that light must be weighted by \(0.4\) to contribute correctly to the pixel.

title: Rendering
section: Why do we even need this thing?
text:
Why do we even need this thing?
We all know that the rendering equation has 2 infinities: infinite dimensions and infinite samples: \[L_{\lambda}^{\text{out}} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{T})^{-1} L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}} = L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}} + \mathbf{R}\mathbf{T} \cdot L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}} + (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{T})^2 \cdot L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit}} + \dots \quad \text{}\] This is a \(2 \times 2 + 2 \times 2 + 2 \times 2 + \dots = \infty\) dimensional integral. It is not like the same BRDF, here we zweig the directions too. So we still sum up, but using Monte Carlo philosophy, using importance sampling to approximate the integral.

VERY IMPORTANT ALERT

For normal integration, error grows with the dimensional-root of the number of samples (\(\epsilon = \Delta f / \sqrt[d]{N}\)). Remember the triangles? yes! => But Monte Carlo error only grows with the square root of the number of samples (\(\sigma[\hat{f}_N] = \sigma_f / \sqrt{N}\)), regardless of the dimension of the integral.

title: Rendering
text:
Formulate Monte Carlo Integration
The ultimate goal is to estimate an integral or expectation value. \[F = \int f(x) dx\] But well, we could not afford to get all samples. So the magic happens when you realize that the integral \(F\) can be estimated by simply taking the average of just a small amount of samples \(N\).
BUT with a condition, weighted each sample by its own probability density function \(p(\mathbf{x})\). \[\hat{F} = \frac{1}{N} \sum \frac{f(x_i)}{p(x_i)}\] Actually, it is: \[\hat{F} = E [\frac{1}{N} \sum \frac{f(x_i)}{p(x_i)}]\]
Because when we calculation multiple times, each time we get different result, and this means we take average of all of these results.
The more samples we have, the smaller the variance \(V[\hat{F}]\) and its standard deviation \(\sigma[\hat{F}]\): \[V[\hat{F}] = \frac{\sigma^2}{N} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma[\hat{F}] = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}\]
Where \(\sigma\) is the real standard deviation of the function \(f(x)\).
And you see what? This solves the curse of dimensionality! That means, the statistical error decreases with the square root of the number of samples (\(O(1/\sqrt{N})\)), regardless of the dimension of the integral.

title: Rendering
text:
Importance Sampling
OK, now we have created the framework - Monte Carlo. But well, we could not afford to get many samples, we need to be a little strategic about what samples to take so that the variance is minimized.
So the ideal case is, the sampling distribution \(p(\mathbf{x})\) is chosen to be proportional to the function \(f(\mathbf{x})\). \[p(\mathbf{x}) \propto f(\mathbf{x})\]
Since \(p(\mathbf{x})\) must be a valid probability density function, the integral over the entire domain \(\Omega\) must be \(1\) (\(\int p(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x} = 1\)). This requires \(p(\mathbf{x})\) to be normalized by the true value of the integral (\(F = \int f(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x}\)): \[p(\mathbf{x}) = c \cdot f(\mathbf{x}), \quad \text{where } c = \frac{1}{F}\] \[p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{f(\mathbf{x})}{F} = \frac{f(\mathbf{x})}{\int f(\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{x}}\]
THIS IS SUPER IMPORTANT, WITHOUT THIS F, THERE WOULD BE NO SUM OF LIGHTS, OTHERWISE WOULD BE ONLY AVERAGED LIGHT

title: Rendering
text:
Example

Step 1: Taking F from BRDF
Let’s say at a bounce the BRDF of incoming light intensity \(f(x)\) from angles between 0 and 2. We guess that the actual light intensity is also similar. \[f(x) = 2x\] First, we must calculate \(F\), basically integrating the BRDF. \[F = \int_{0}^{2} f(x) \, dx = \int_{0}^{2} 2x \, dx\] \[F = [x^2]_0^2 = 2^2 - 0^2 = \mathbf{4}\] BRDF is 4. If our Monte Carlo algorithm is good and the light really has the same intensity as BRDF, it will give us 4.


Step 2: Importance Sampling PDF from BRDF
Now, we are going to build our Probability Density Function \(p(x)\) using your exact formula: \(p(x) = \frac{f(x)}{F}\).Since we know \(f(x) = 2x\) and \(F = 4\), we plug them in: \[p(x) = \frac{2x}{4} = \frac{x}{2}\]


Step 3: The Monte Carlo Estimate
What else, just plug numbers in: \[\hat{F} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{f(x_i)}{p(x_i)}\]
Let’s take just one single random sample (\(N=1\)).
Scenario A: The computer randomly picks \(x = 1.5\) \[\hat{F} = \frac{f(1.5)}{p(1.5)} = \frac{3}{0.75} = \mathbf{4}\]
Result: 4. With just ONE single ray, we got the exact perfect answer.
Scenario B: The computer randomly picks a different number, \(x = 0.5\) \[\hat{F} = \frac{f(0.5)}{p(0.5)} = \frac{1}{0.25} = \mathbf{4}\]
Result: 4.
In reality, we actually dont know \(F\), so we “guess” a \(p(x)\) that looks similar to \(f(x)\) (mostly using the BRDF lobe), but it won’t be perfectly proportional. Because it’s not perfect, the math won’t cancel out cleanly to 4 every single time. Sometimes you’ll get 3.8, sometimes 4.2. Therefore we do it many times and take the average.

title: Rendering
text:
Depth of Field
So we want to model the blurriness of the lens. pixels are only sharp when they are placed at focal plane, if they are placed a little front or back, it becomes blur. And for a pixel, we integrate over all point x on the pixel area on this focal plane, and over allapeture area. \[\Phi_{\lambda, j} = \mathbf{M}_{\lambda, j}^{\Omega^{\text{in}}(\mathbf{x}) \times A_j} L_{\lambda}^{\text{in}} \quad \text{}\] * \(\Phi_{\lambda, j}\) total spectral power (flux) for pixel \(j\) (brightness) * \(\mathbf{M}_{\lambda, j}^{\Omega^{\text{in}}(\mathbf{x}) \times A_j}\) (The Measurement Operator) * \(A_j\): The integral is performed over the pixel area defined on the focal plane. * \(\Omega^{\text{in}}(\mathbf{x})\): The integral is performed over the solid angle of directions that pass through the lens aperture (\(a\)) and contribute to the point \(\mathbf{x}\) on the focal plane. * \(L_{\lambda}^{\text{in}}\) is just incoming light solved by the Rendering Equation

title: Rendering
text:
Spatial Filtering
Goal: When a ray tracer calculates the color of a pixel, it draws multiple samples (light paths) within the pixel area. If these samples are simply averaged without weighting, it corresponds to a box filter. However, it can create Aliasing idk why.
To fix, it is really simple! Weight the samples using a Gaußian filter. This filter weights samples closer to the pixel center more heavily than samples near the edges. \[\text{Pixel Color} = \int_{-1}^{1} L(x) \cdot k(x) \, dx\]

title: Rendering
text:
Temporal Filtering
Goal: when the camera’s shutter is open for a finite amount of time, causing moving objects to appear stretched.
This is done in the same way as spatial filtering, but instead of integrating over the pixel area (\(x, y\)), the renderer integrates over a time interval. For example, a pixel might see different part of a moving ball at different timestamp when it emits the exact same ray direction!

title: Rendering
text:
Russian Roulette
At each bounce, a random decision is made to either continue tracing the path or terminate it.

title: Rendering
text:
Example 1 only One Strategy (no MIS)
Once again, Monte Carlo with importance sampling but without MIS: \[L_{\lambda}^{\text{reflect}}(\omega^{\text{out}}) \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{\rho(\omega_i^{\text{in}}, \omega^{\text{out}})L_{\lambda}^{\text{in}}(\omega_i^{\text{in}})\cos\theta_i^{\text{in}}}{p(\omega_i^{\text{in}})} \quad \text{}\]
We are going to shoot \(N = 2\) random rays out from a diffuse floor. BRDF is \(\rho = \frac{0.5}{\pi}\) (Albedo of diffuse material always divide by \(\pi\) to conserve energy). \[p(\omega_i^{\text{in}}) = \frac{\cos\theta_i^{\text{in}}}{\pi}\] we sample the light based on Nusselt’s analogy.
Ray 1 hits \(L^{\text{in}}_1\) with 100 unit of light, and angle \(\cos\theta_1\) of 60 degrees. \[\text{Value}_1 = \frac{\rho \cdot L^{\text{in}}_1 \cdot \cos\theta_1}{p(\omega_1)} = \frac{\frac{0.5}{\pi} \cdot 100 \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{\cos\theta_1}{\pi}} = 50\]
Ray 2 hits \(L^{\text{in}}_2\) dark ceiling with 10 unit of light, and angle \(\cos\theta_2\) of 0 degrees. \[\text{Value}_2 = \frac{\rho \cdot L^{\text{in}}_2 \cdot \cos\theta_2}{p(\omega_2)} = \frac{\frac{0.5}{\pi} \cdot 10 \cdot 0}{\frac{\cos\theta_2}{\pi}} = 5\]
Average: \[\text{Average} = \frac{\text{Value}_1 + \text{Value}_2}{2} = \frac{50 + 5}{2} = 27.5\]

title: Rendering
text:
Example 2 with more Strategies (with MIS)
Now we use MIS to combine two strategies (2 rays) at the exact same time: Direct Light Sampling and BRDF Sampling. We add an extra weight before each light. THIS EXTRA WEIGHT IS ALL ABOUT THE TRUST TO THAT METHOD \[\hat{f}^N_{\text{MIS}} = \sum_{i=1}^m \frac{1}{n_i} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} \omega_i(x_{i,j}) \frac{f(x_{i,j})}{p_i(x_{i,j})} \quad \text{}\] \[\hat{f}^N_{\text{MIS}} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{n_1} \sum_{j=1}^{n_1} \omega_1(x_{1,j}) \frac{f(x_{1,j})}{p_1(x_{1,j})}}_{\text{Contribution from Strategy 1 (e.g., Light Sampling)}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{n_2} \sum_{j=1}^{n_2} \omega_2(x_{2,j}) \frac{f(x_{2,j})}{p_2(x_{2,j})}}_{\text{Contribution from Strategy 2 (e.g., BRDF Sampling)}} + \dots\]
For Path Tracing: \[\text{Final Color} = \left[ w_{\text{light}} \cdot \frac{f(x_1)}{p_{\text{light}}(x_1)} \right] + \left[ w_{\text{brdf}} \cdot \frac{f(x_2)}{p_{\text{brdf}}(x_2)} \right]\] where \(f = \rho \cdot L^{\text{in}} \cdot \cos\theta\)

Ray 1: Direct Light Sampling
A random point on a light bulb is picked, the light intensity \(f = \mathbf{90}\) and:

\(p_{\text{light}}\): The probability that the Light Sampler picked this spot is 0.4 (because aiming at the light is its only job).
\(p_{\text{brdf}}\): The probability that the BRDF Sampler would have randomly fired in this dull direction is very low, just 0.05.

\[L_{\text{direct}} = \frac{p_{\text{light}}}{p_{\text{light}} + p_{\text{brdf}}} \cdot \frac{f(x_1)}{p_{\text{light}}(x_1)} = \frac{0.4}{0.4 + 0.05} \cdot \frac{90}{0.4} = \frac{0.4}{0.45} \cdot 225 = 0.888 * 225 = \mathbf{200}\]


Ray 2: BRDF Sampling
Second ray follows BRDF lobe and accidentally got \(f = \mathbf{400}\):

\(p_{\text{light}}\): The probability that the Light Sampler would have picked this exact sub-pixel coordinate on the lamp is lower, say 0.14.
\(p_{\text{brdf}}\): The probability that the BRDF Sampler fired here is 0.35 (because it heavily favors shiny angles).

\[L_{\text{brdf}} = \frac{p_{\text{brdf}}}{p_{\text{light}} + p_{\text{brdf}}} \cdot \frac{f(x_2)}{p_{\text{brdf}}(x_2)} = \frac{0.35}{0.14 + 0.35} \cdot \frac{400}{0.35} = \frac{0.35}{0.49} \cdot \frac{400}{0.35} = \frac{400}{0.49} \approx \mathbf{816}\]
To get the final color, we just add the weighted contributions from our two strategies together: \[\text{Final Color} = L_{\text{direct}} + L_{\text{brdf}} = 200 + 816 = \mathbf{1016}\]

title: Rendering
text:
Indirect light sampling (keyword: cosin & BRDF)
To calculate indirect light: \[L_\lambda^{\text{indirect}}(x, \omega^{\text{out}}) = \iint_{\Omega^{\text{in}}} \rho(x, \omega^{\text{in}}, \omega^{\text{out}}) L_\lambda^{\text{reflect}}(\text{trace}(x, \omega^{\text{in}}), -\omega^{\text{in}}) \cos\theta^{\text{in}} d\Omega^{\text{in}}\]

1. BRDF sampling
I think we all know what that is, quite intuitive that we want to sample according to BRDF lobe. But let’s discuss about this in another post.


2. Cosine sampling
Area of a unit circle is \(\pi r^2 = \pi (1)^2 = \pi\), so PDF is \(p(x, y) = 1/\pi\). We use this as the base to calculate PDF of each direction \(p_{\text{Nusselt}}(\omega^{in}) = \cos\theta^{in}/\pi\).
Imagine we sample N random direction, this is our Monte Carlo estimation: \[L_{\lambda,\text{Nusselt}}^{\text{reflect}}(\omega^{out}) \approx\frac{1}{N} \sum \frac{\rho(\omega_i^{in}, \omega^{out}) \cdot L_\lambda^{in}(\omega_i^{in}) \cdot \cos\theta^{in}}{\left(\frac{\cos\theta^{in}}{\pi}\right)}\] \[L_{\lambda,\text{Nusselt}}^{\text{reflect}}(\omega^{out}) \approx \frac{\pi}{N} \sum_i \rho(\omega_i^{in}, \omega^{out}) L_\lambda^{in}(\omega_i^{in})\]

title: Rendering
text:
Direct light sampling (keyword: Area)
FOr direct light sampling we rewrite into integral over areas: \[L_{\lambda,l}^{\text{direct,area}}(x, \omega^{\text{out}}) = \iint_{y \in A_l} \rho \cdot L_{\lambda,l}^{\text{emit,area}} \cdot V(x, y) \frac{\cos\theta^{x \to y} \cos\theta^{y \to x}}{\|x - y\|^2} dA_y\]
We can also estimate this using Monte Carlo:

For each area light \(l\), we need to sample a number \(N_l\) of points \(\mathbf{y}_i\) on the light source.
The integral is then approximated by a sum, where each sample \(\mathbf{y}_i\) is weighted by \(1/p(\mathbf{y}_i)\).
\(p(\mathbf{y}_i)\): The Probability Density Function for sampling a point \(\mathbf{y}_i\) on the area light. \[L_{\lambda}^{\text{direct,area}}(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\text{out}}) \approx \frac{1}{N_l} \sum_{i=1}^{N_l} \frac{\rho(\mathbf{x}, \omega^{\mathbf{x} \to \mathbf{y}_i}, \omega^{\text{out}}) L_{\lambda}^{\text{emit,area}}(\mathbf{y}_i, \omega^{\mathbf{y}_i \to \mathbf{x}}) G(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}_i)}{p(\mathbf{y}_i)} \quad \text{}\]

title: Polygonal Mesh
text:
mesh 𝑀 is defined as a tripel (𝑉, 𝐸, 𝐹)
Topological type using betti numbers
incident: different type, adjacent: same type
valence (given a vertex: number of incident edge/traingles?)
degree (given a face: number of incident edges?)
\(\sum valences = 2edges = \sum degrees\) (because mostly one edge is shared by 2 vertices and 2 faces)
winding order is used for backface culling (when we add into the program)



v -1 -1 1
v 1 -1 1
v -1 1 1
...
vt u1 v1
vt u2 v2
vt u3 v3
...
vn -1 0 0
vn 1 0 0
vn 0 -1 0
...
f 1/1/1 3/3/1 7/?/1 5/?/1
f 2/2/2 6/6/2 8//2 4//2
...

deep in hardware could be saved as:

array: very fast for linear traversal, add and delete slower
double linked-list: add and delete faster, but linear traversal slower





Goal idea: resembling 2D plane everywhere on the surface:

each edge is incident to 1 or 2 faces (if less: no face, if more: no clear surface definition, if 1: boundary)
each edge is incident to 2 vertices (no rounded edge with its face in itself)
each face is bounded by 1 loop over incident edges (if not: its a hole)
each vertex: the incident faces and edges form a single “fan” which is either open (on the boundary) or a closed disk (in the interior of the mesh), but not multiple fans.

DS are mostly restricted on this because:

Reliably deterministic in operation (e.g. each edge only has 1,2 faces at 2 sides which create a clear surface for traversal) => avoid ambigioiusity
Naturally it is able to model most of 3D shapes





classify all corners in convex or concave (already knew from internal angles)
repeat n-3 times: (n loop)

one ear-cut: iterate all convex corners (corner hear means vertex and the edges at two sides): (n loop)

check, if corner is an ear (if it contains no concave inside the border line)
if yes, cut-off ear, reclassify adjacent corners, break this loop to go to the next eat-cut







Triangulated using Ear-Cutting
\(2e = 3f\) or \(\frac{3}{2} f = e\) bc each faces has 3 edges, but each edge is used double
Euler formula: \(v-e+f=X\), for X very small => \(v = \frac{1}{3} e = \frac{1}{2} f\)
valence: \(\sum valences = 2e = 6v\)








For triangle: \(\hat{n} = (p_1 - p_0) \times (p_2 - p_0)\) and \(A = \frac{1}{2} \left| p_0 \times p_1 + p_1 \times p_2 + p_2 \times p_0 \right|\)
For Polygon: \(\hat{n} \propto (p_0 \times p_1) + (p_1 \times p_2) + \ldots + (p_{d-1} \times p_0)\) and \(A = \frac{1}{2} \left| (p_0 \times p_1) + (p_1 \times p_2) + \ldots + (p_{d-1} \times p_0) \right|\)

General idea for Area:

Choose a reference point, typically the origin (0, 0).
Iterate through each directed edges (p_i, p_j) of the polygon, calculate the signed area of the triangle it forms with the origin using the z-component of the cross product: \(\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} (x_i y_j - x_j y_i)\). (It is represented by a normal pointing inside in the winding order aka right-hand rule, some shorter, some longer will sum up to the correct direction)
Sum all these signed areas \(A = \sum \Delta A\). Concave (negative area) and Convex (positive area)

Advantage:

No need ear-cutting: O(n²)
Taking care of concave triangles




Idea comes from Divergence Theorem: sum of all the “sources” and “sinks” within a volume equal to the total amount of “stuff” flowing out (or in) through the surface boundary of that volume. \(\iint_S \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = \iiint_V (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}) dV\)
Where:

\(\mathbf{F}\) is the vector field.
\(S\) is a closed surface.
\(V\) is the volume enclosed by \(S\).
\(d\mathbf{S}\) is an infinitesimal outward normal vector element of the surface.
\(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}\) is the divergence of the vector field \(\mathbf{F}\).

In simpler terms:

Flux (\(\iint_S \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{S}\)): This represents the “flow” of the vector field out of the enclosed volume through its boundary surface. Imagine a fluid flowing; the flux would be the amount of fluid passing through the surface per unit time.
Divergence (\(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}\)): This measures the “source” or “sink” density of the vector field at each point within the volume. A positive divergence indicates a source (where the field is originating or expanding), and a negative divergence indicates a sink (where the field is converging or disappearing).

Now we apply that to Volume, each tetrahedron volume created by each triangle \(\frac{1}{6} \mathbf{p}_i \cdot (\mathbf{p}_j \times \mathbf{p}_k)\) (also called triple product)
Then the total volume \(V\) is sum of tetrahedron volume: \(V = \frac{1}{6} \sum_{(ijk) \in T} [\mathbf{p}_i, \mathbf{p}_j, \mathbf{p}_k]\)



Let’s start with the volume contribution of a tetrahedron formed by the origin and a triangle \((\mathbf{p}_i, \mathbf{p}_j, \mathbf{p}_k)\):
\(V_{ijk} = \frac{1}{6} \mathbf{p}_k \cdot (\mathbf{p}_i \times \mathbf{p}_j)\)
\(\frac{\partial V_{ijk}}{\partial \mathbf{p}_k} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{p}_k} \left( \frac{1}{6} \mathbf{p}_i \cdot (\mathbf{p}_j \times \mathbf{p}_k) \right)\) (volume with respect to the coordinates of a specific vertex \(\frac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{p}_k}\))
\(\frac{\partial V_{ijk}}{\partial \mathbf{p}_k} = \frac{1}{6} (\mathbf{p}_i \times \mathbf{p}_j)\)
Until now what this means?

It means much the volume of that specific tetrahedron changes if we move vertex \(\mathbf{p}_k\) by a small amount.
The result, \(\frac{1}{6} (\mathbf{p}_i \times \mathbf{p}_j)\), is a vector. This vector is normal to the plane formed by \(\mathbf{p}_i\) and \(\mathbf{p}_j\), and its magnitude is related to the area of the triangle formed by \(\mathbf{p}_i\), \(\mathbf{p}_j\), and the origin.

For the total volume \(V\), the gradient with respect to a vertex \(\mathbf{p}_k\) is the sum of the gradients of all tetrahedra one-ring around \(\mathbf{p}_k\):
\(\nabla_{\mathbf{p}_k} V = \sum_{\text{one-ring } (\mathbf{p}_i, \mathbf{p}_j, \mathbf{p}_k)} \frac{1}{6} (\mathbf{p}_i \times \mathbf{p}_j)\)
This gradient indicates the direction in which moving the vertex would most effectively increase the total mesh volume.



\(\hat{n}_v \propto \sum_i w_i \hat{n}_i\)
Implementation is interestingly reversed: because faces and vertices are jointly incident to each other
normals.resize(positions.size()); // init normals array with length equal to number of vertices
foreach (fi in meshfaces) // iterate over all faces
  nml = get_face_normal(fi); // get the normal for this face
  foreach (corner ci in face fi) // iterate over all vertices of this face
    normals[vi(ci)] += nml *compute_corner_weight(ci); // add the weighted normal to the corresponding normal array index



\(c_v = \frac{1}{n_{onering}} \sum_i p_i\) Implementaton also a little reversed, hell yeah, because all vertices are adjacent to each other
centroids.resize(positions.size()); //init
valences.resize(positions.size());
foreach (fi in meshfaces) //for each face
  foreach (halfedge vi,vj in face fi) // iterate three halfedges (vi,vj)
    centroids[vi] += positions[vj];
    valences[vi] += 1;







Shells are connected components
Boundary Loops are border/edges




This DS can be used to find shells three operations: * initialize UF-DS such that each element forms its own subset * union(element1, element2): Merges the sets containing the two elements. * find(element): Returns a unique identifier for the set that the element belongs to.
Algorithm to find shells and count shells
int nr_comp = vertices.size(); 
UF.init(vertices.size()); // init to component/set per vertex
foreach (fi) // iterate face
  foreach (halfedge vi,vj in face fi) //iterate HE
  if (UF.find(vi) != UF.find(vj))
    UF.union(vi,vj); //unite
    nr_comp -= 1; //reduce comp by 1

array: very fast for linear traversal, add and delete slower
double linked-list: add and delete faster, but linear traversal slower





Goal idea: resembling 2D plane everywhere on the surface:

each edge is incident to 1 or 2 faces (if less: no face, if more: no clear surface definition, if 1: boundary)
each edge is incident to 2 vertices (no rounded edge with its face in itself)
each face is bounded by 1 loop over incident edges (if not: its a hole)
each vertex: the incident faces and edges form a single “fan” which is either open (on the boundary) or a closed disk (in the interior of the mesh), but not multiple fans.

DS are mostly restricted on this because:

Reliably deterministic in operation (e.g. each edge only has 1,2 faces at 2 sides which create a clear surface for traversal) => avoid ambigioiusity
Naturally it is able to model most of 3D shapes





classify all corners in convex or concave (already knew from internal angles)
repeat n-3 times: (n loop)

one ear-cut: iterate all convex corners (corner hear means vertex and the edges at two sides): (n loop)

check, if corner is an ear (if it contains no concave inside the border line)
if yes, cut-off ear, reclassify adjacent corners, break this loop to go to the next eat-cut







Triangulated using Ear-Cutting
\(2e = 3f\) or \(\frac{3}{2} f = e\) bc each faces has 3 edges, but each edge is used double
Euler formula: \(v-e+f=X\), for X very small => \(v = \frac{1}{3} e = \frac{1}{2} f\)
valence: \(\sum valences = 2e = 6v\)

title: Polygonal Mesh
text:
For triangle: \(\hat{n} = (p_1 - p_0) \times (p_2 - p_0)\) and \(A = \frac{1}{2} \left| p_0 \times p_1 + p_1 \times p_2 + p_2 \times p_0 \right|\)
For Polygon: \(\hat{n} \propto (p_0 \times p_1) + (p_1 \times p_2) + \ldots + (p_{d-1} \times p_0)\) and \(A = \frac{1}{2} \left| (p_0 \times p_1) + (p_1 \times p_2) + \ldots + (p_{d-1} \times p_0) \right|\)

General idea for Area:

Choose a reference point, typically the origin (0, 0).
Iterate through each directed edges (p_i, p_j) of the polygon, calculate the signed area of the triangle it forms with the origin using the z-component of the cross product: \(\Delta A = \frac{1}{2} (x_i y_j - x_j y_i)\). (It is represented by a normal pointing inside in the winding order aka right-hand rule, some shorter, some longer will sum up to the correct direction)
Sum all these signed areas \(A = \sum \Delta A\). Concave (negative area) and Convex (positive area)

Advantage:

No need ear-cutting: O(n²)
Taking care of concave triangles




Idea comes from Divergence Theorem: sum of all the “sources” and “sinks” within a volume equal to the total amount of “stuff” flowing out (or in) through the surface boundary of that volume. \(\iint_S \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = \iiint_V (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}) dV\)
Where:

\(\mathbf{F}\) is the vector field.
\(S\) is a closed surface.
\(V\) is the volume enclosed by \(S\).
\(d\mathbf{S}\) is an infinitesimal outward normal vector element of the surface.
\(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}\) is the divergence of the vector field \(\mathbf{F}\).

In simpler terms:

Flux (\(\iint_S \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{S}\)): This represents the “flow” of the vector field out of the enclosed volume through its boundary surface. Imagine a fluid flowing; the flux would be the amount of fluid passing through the surface per unit time.
Divergence (\(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}\)): This measures the “source” or “sink” density of the vector field at each point within the volume. A positive divergence indicates a source (where the field is originating or expanding), and a negative divergence indicates a sink (where the field is converging or disappearing).

Now we apply that to Volume, each tetrahedron volume created by each triangle \(\frac{1}{6} \mathbf{p}_i \cdot (\mathbf{p}_j \times \mathbf{p}_k)\) (also called triple product)
Then the total volume \(V\) is sum of tetrahedron volume: \(V = \frac{1}{6} \sum_{(ijk) \in T} [\mathbf{p}_i, \mathbf{p}_j, \mathbf{p}_k]\)



Let’s start with the volume contribution of a tetrahedron formed by the origin and a triangle \((\mathbf{p}_i, \mathbf{p}_j, \mathbf{p}_k)\):
\(V_{ijk} = \frac{1}{6} \mathbf{p}_k \cdot (\mathbf{p}_i \times \mathbf{p}_j)\)
\(\frac{\partial V_{ijk}}{\partial \mathbf{p}_k} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{p}_k} \left( \frac{1}{6} \mathbf{p}_i \cdot (\mathbf{p}_j \times \mathbf{p}_k) \right)\) (volume with respect to the coordinates of a specific vertex \(\frac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{p}_k}\))
\(\frac{\partial V_{ijk}}{\partial \mathbf{p}_k} = \frac{1}{6} (\mathbf{p}_i \times \mathbf{p}_j)\)
Until now what this means?

It means much the volume of that specific tetrahedron changes if we move vertex \(\mathbf{p}_k\) by a small amount.
The result, \(\frac{1}{6} (\mathbf{p}_i \times \mathbf{p}_j)\), is a vector. This vector is normal to the plane formed by \(\mathbf{p}_i\) and \(\mathbf{p}_j\), and its magnitude is related to the area of the triangle formed by \(\mathbf{p}_i\), \(\mathbf{p}_j\), and the origin.

For the total volume \(V\), the gradient with respect to a vertex \(\mathbf{p}_k\) is the sum of the gradients of all tetrahedra one-ring around \(\mathbf{p}_k\):
\(\nabla_{\mathbf{p}_k} V = \sum_{\text{one-ring } (\mathbf{p}_i, \mathbf{p}_j, \mathbf{p}_k)} \frac{1}{6} (\mathbf{p}_i \times \mathbf{p}_j)\)
This gradient indicates the direction in which moving the vertex would most effectively increase the total mesh volume.



\(\hat{n}_v \propto \sum_i w_i \hat{n}_i\)
Implementation is interestingly reversed: because faces and vertices are jointly incident to each other
normals.resize(positions.size()); // init normals array with length equal to number of vertices
foreach (fi in meshfaces) // iterate over all faces
  nml = get_face_normal(fi); // get the normal for this face
  foreach (corner ci in face fi) // iterate over all vertices of this face
    normals[vi(ci)] += nml *compute_corner_weight(ci); // add the weighted normal to the corresponding normal array index



\(c_v = \frac{1}{n_{onering}} \sum_i p_i\) Implementaton also a little reversed, hell yeah, because all vertices are adjacent to each other
centroids.resize(positions.size()); //init
valences.resize(positions.size());
foreach (fi in meshfaces) //for each face
  foreach (halfedge vi,vj in face fi) // iterate three halfedges (vi,vj)
    centroids[vi] += positions[vj];
    valences[vi] += 1;

title: Polygonal Mesh
text:
Shells are connected components
Boundary Loops are border/edges




This DS can be used to find shells three operations: * initialize UF-DS such that each element forms its own subset * union(element1, element2): Merges the sets containing the two elements. * find(element): Returns a unique identifier for the set that the element belongs to.
Algorithm to find shells and count shells
int nr_comp = vertices.size(); 
UF.init(vertices.size()); // init to component/set per vertex
foreach (fi) // iterate face
  foreach (halfedge vi,vj in face fi) //iterate HE
  if (UF.find(vi) != UF.find(vj))
    UF.union(vi,vj); //unite
    nr_comp -= 1; //reduce comp by 1

title: Polygonal Mesh
Polygonal Mesh
text:
Mesh Analysis

Design Pattern for Design Mesh Processing Algorithm

Circulator (around vertex, face, vertex in face)
Tagging (mark processed elements or store id/flag)
Region Growing (triangle strip, compressed segmentation)



Why use Triangle Strips for Rendering?

Transfer: only need to transfer n+2 vertices for a strip of n triangles (the first one all 3, after that only 1 for each new triangle)
Access: for triangle mesh we all know \(f = 2v\), triangle strip can utilize this optimum, but without strip, each face individually require separate 3 times access to their vertices. So \(f = 2.3v\)
Downside? The algorithm is not always perfect (its just greedy)



How to compute Triangle Strip?
Output Goal: strip index per face 1. Sample some seed 2. For each seed, generate a stripification 3. Here is how to stripification: * 2 types of HE, (even - odd) using parity (0-1) depending on how the origin aligned. * Forward: prev(inv(vi)) or next(inv(vi)) * Backward: … This run alternatively…change parity 0-1-0-1-0-1 Until Border or Comeback to beginning 4. Choose the longest strip from seed 5. Repeat with new set of seeds


How to do Orientability Check?
Region Growing: Start at a seed to queue 1. Take the first face from queue 2. Check if orientable? 1. check if all neighboring processed faces have consistent orientation 2. If no, give up. But if yes, swap next and inv 3. Add all neighbors to queue

title: Polygonal Mesh
text:
Understanding the Euler Characteristic

1. Basic Definition
For any closed (borderless) surface S subdivided into vertices (V), edges (E), and faces (F):
\[
\chi(S) = V - E + F
\]
Key Property: - χ is a topological invariant - it depends only on the surface’s “shape” (topology), not on how we subdivide it.


2. Examples



Surface
V - E + F
χ




Sphere
8-12+6 = 2 (cube)  4-6+4 = 2 (tetrahedron)
2


Torus
16-32+16 = 0
0


Double Torus
8-16+8 = 0
0 ?





3. Topological Meaning
The Euler characteristic can be expressed via Betti numbers (counting topological features):
\[
\chi = h_0 - h_1 + h_2
\]

\(h_0\): 0-Dimensional Holes (Connected Components)

Counts the number of separate pieces in the surface
For a single connected surface: \(h_0 = 1\)
Example: Both a sphere and torus have \(h_0 = 1\)



\(h_1\): 1-Dimensional Holes (Non-Trivial Loops)

Counts the maximum number of independent closed curves that:

Cannot be shrunk to a point
Don’t split the surface when cut

Sphere: \(h_1 = 0\) (all loops can shrink)
Torus: \(h_1 = 2\) (meridian and longitude loops)
Double Torus: \(h_1 = 4\)



\(h_2\): Number of orientable shells



Surface
\(h_0\)
\(h_1\)
\(h_2\)
\(\chi\)




Sphere
1
0
1
2


Torus
1
2
1
0


Disk
1
0
0
1






5. Geometric Interpretation

Positive \(\chi\): Sphere-like topology
Zero \(\chi\): Torus-like topology
Negative \(\chi\): Higher-genus surfaces (multiple handles)



5. Practical Applications

Mesh Processing:

Verify mesh integrity (e.g., χ=2 for watertight meshes)
Detect topological changes during simplification

Physics:

Gauss-Bonnet theorem relates χ to total curvature
Essential in string theory and cosmology

Computer Graphics:

Texture mapping requires χ=2 for spherical topology
Character modeling (ear/handles change topology)

title: Physics Simulation
text:
Hooke’s Law \(F = -kx\) (\(k\) is stiffness constant)
Newton 2nd Law \(F = ma = m\ddot{x} = -kx\)

So Original equation is \[m\ddot{x} + kx = 0\]
Now we add \(B\dot{x}\) term (damping force) It creates a force that is proportional to the velocity (\(dx/dt\)). The faster you move, the harder the “air” pushes back against you \[m\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + kx = 0\]
Then we add actual A (unormalized actual strength unlike \(F(t) = \sin(t)\) only between -1 and 1) \[m\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + kx = A(t)\]


Idea Because the acceleration \(\ddot{x}\) is proportional to the negative position \(-x\), the solution is a sine/cosine wave. \[x(t) = A \cdot \cos(\omega t + \phi)\] \[\dot{x}(t) = -\omega A \cdot \sin(\omega t + \phi)\] \[\ddot{x}(t) = -\omega^2 A \cdot \cos(\omega t + \phi)\]



Let’s create a state vector and Euler method: \[\vec{y} = \begin{pmatrix} x \\ v \equiv \dot{x} \end{pmatrix} \quad \vec{y}_{i+1} = \vec{y}_i + h\dot{\vec{y}}_i\]
So where to find \(\dot{\vec{y}}_i\)? We have observed that: \(\dot{x} = v\) and \(\dot{v} = -\omega^2 x\), just ignore the \(v\) \[\dot{\vec{y}}_i = \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{v} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\omega^2 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ v \end{pmatrix}\]






Base units: Kelvin, meter, kg, second, candela,… How to find unit of a constant? Example: \[m \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + B \frac{dx}{dt} + kx = F(t)\] Each term in this equation represents a force, so every term must also be Force: \(\text{kg} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{-2}\) (Newtons). Now we can find unit of \(B\) damping consant: \[[B] = \frac{[F(t)]}{[dx/dt]} = \frac{\text{kg} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{-2}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}} = \text{kg} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}\]
Candela & Luminous Intensity The Candela (\(cd\)) is the SI unit of luminous intensity, defined as the luminous intensity of a monochromatic source of light emitting radiation of frequency 540 THz (green light) with a radiant intensity of 1/683 W/sr.
Solid Angle (\(\Omega\)) (Unit: steradians, \(sr\)): measures the “area” covered by a set of directions on the surface of a unit sphere \[\Omega = A / r^2\]



Substituting variables so that the resulting equations are governed by all pure numbers


\[m \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + B \frac{dx}{dt} + kx = AF(t)\]
Redefine variables time (\(t_c\)) and position (\(x_c\)) and \(\tau\) and \(\chi\) are all scales of \(t\) and \(x\). So we replace every \(t = \tau t_c\) and every \(x = \chi x_c\). \[m \frac{d^2(\chi x_c)}{(d\tau t_c)^2} + B \frac{d(\chi x_c)}{d\tau t_c} + k(\chi x_c) = AF(t)\] \[m \frac{x_c}{t_c^2} \frac{d^2\chi}{d\tau^2} + B \frac{x_c}{t_c} \frac{d\chi}{d\tau} + k x_c \chi = AF(t)\]
Divide the entire equation by \(m \frac{x_c}{t_c^2}\): \[\frac{d^2\chi}{d\tau^2} + t_c \frac{B}{m} \frac{d\chi}{d\tau} + t_c^2 \frac{k}{m} \chi = \frac{t_c^2}{mx_c} Af(\tau)\]
Choose the Smart Constants. To make it simple, we choose \(t_c\) and \(x_c\) based on the system’s own properties:

Characteristic Time: \(t_c = \sqrt{m/k}\) (the inverse of the natural frequency).
Characteristic Position: \(x_c = A/k\) (the static displacement under force \(A\)).

But WTF why so random? what are the principles? have a look agai, our goal is make as many terms to 1 as possible: \[\frac{d^2\chi}{d\tau^2} + \underbrace{\left( t_c \frac{B}{m} \right)}_{\text{Term 1}} \frac{d\chi}{d\tau} + \underbrace{\left( t_c^2 \frac{k}{m} \right)}_{\text{Term 2}} \chi = \underbrace{\left( \frac{t_c^2 A}{m x_c} \right)}_{\text{Term 3}} f(\tau)\] Look at Term 2, isn’t it convenient when we have \(m\) is in kg, \(k\) is in kg/s², how about we take \(t_c^2 = \frac{m}{k}\) to make everything 1 and also the units cancel each other out? Yeah let’s do it.
Now we substitute \(t_c\) in Term 3 (\(\frac{t_c^2 A}{m x_c}\)). We already decided \(t_c^2 = m/k\), so let’s plug that in: \[\left( \frac{(m/k) A}{m x_c} \right) \implies \frac{A}{k x_c}\]. To make this term 1, we must set \(x_c = A/k\).
Now we substitute \(t_c\) in Term 1 (\(\frac{t_c B}{m}\)). We already decided \(t_c^2 = m/k\), so let’s plug that in: \[\sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} \cdot \frac{B}{m} = \frac{B}{\sqrt{mk}}\] This “leftover” becomes the Damping Ratio (\(2\zeta\))
Once again, everything left is dimensionless: \[\frac{d^2\chi}{d\tau^2} + 2\zeta \frac{d\chi}{d\tau} + \chi = f(\tau)\] Where the damping ratio is defined as: \[2\zeta = \frac{B}{\sqrt{mk}}\]
But why?

Reason 1: If now I say set \(B = 10\), you have no idea if the system will wobble or not When we nondimensionalize, we find that the behavior doesn’t depend on \(B\) alone, but on the ratio \(B / \sqrt{mk}\).
Reason 2: All the variables now regarding the time or positions are \(t_c^2 = \frac{m}{k}\) and \(x_c = \frac{A}{k}\), they are:

the dimensions are canceled out, so they are just parameter
they are on the scales (axes) of these variables, not the scale of second or meter anymore







2 ways to calculate in physics:

Field-based: You look at fixed points in space (like a grid) and measure how properties like velocity or density change at those specific locations as time passes. Easy to implement for things like smoke or large-scale fluid volumes. e.g: each grid has a \(\rho(x,y,z)\) and \(v(x,y,z)\)
Particle-based: You follow individual “pieces” of matter as they move through space, “conceptually simple” because you just track the position and mass of each particle. e.g: each particle has a \(m\) and \(v\) and \(p\)
Field -> Particle: randomly sample particles based on the density.
Particle -> Field: sum all particles in a grid cell to get the density.



What is force? negative gradient of a potential energy field \(\phi(\underline{x})\): \[\vec{F} = -\vec{\nabla}_{\underline{x}} \phi(\underline{x})\]
What is Work (\(W\))? energy transferred by a force acting over a path \(\underline{x}(t)\): \[W = \int_{\underline{x}_0}^{\underline{x}_1} \vec{F} d\underline{x}\] A force is conservative if and only if its curl vanishes everywhere (\(\vec{0}\)): \[\vec{\nabla}_{\underline{x}} \times \vec{F} = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z} \\ \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x} \\ \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y} \end{pmatrix} = \vec{0}\]



From Potential Energy (\(\phi\)) to Force (\(\vec{F}\))We start with a given potential energy function:\[\phi(\underline{x}) = x^2 + 2xy - y^2\]
The forces are negative partial derivatives for each component (\(F_x\) and \(F_y\)): \[F_x = -\partial_x \phi = -2x - 2y\] \[F_y = -\partial_y \phi = -2x + 2y\] \[\vec{F} = -2 \begin{pmatrix} x+y \\ x-y \end{pmatrix}\]
Force is conservative only if its curl is zero. Let’s check it: \[\vec{\nabla}_{\underline{x}} \times \vec{F} = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z} \\ \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x} \\ \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 - 0 \\ 0 - 0 \\ -2 - (-2) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\]
Reconstruct Potential Energy from Force Given the force field:\[\vec{F} = \begin{pmatrix} -2x - 2y \\ -2x + 2y \end{pmatrix}\]

We know that \(F_x = -\partial_x \phi\) and \(F_y = -\partial_y \phi\). We can integrate each component separately to find the potential energy function \(\phi(x, y)\).
Integrate \(F_x\) with respect to \(x\) (treating \(y\) as a constant): \[\phi(x, y) = -\int F_x dx = -\int (-2x - 2y) dx = x^2 + 2xy + C_1(y)\]
Integrate \(F_y\) with respect to \(y\) (treating \(x\) as a constant): \[\phi(x, y) = -\int F_y dy = -\int (-2x + 2y) dy = 2xy - y^2 + C_2(x)\]
We have two expressions for \(\phi(x, y)\). Let’s compare them: \[\phi(x, y) = x^2 + 2xy + C_1(y) = 2xy - y^2 + C_2(x)\] \[C_1(y) = C - y^2\] \[C_2(x) = C + x^2\] \[\phi(x, y) = x^2 + 2xy - y^2 + C\]




Solid angle is the “area” covered by a set of directions on the surface of a unit sphere. Unit is steradians (\(sr\)). A full sphere is \(4\pi\) sr.
To define solid angle better, let’s start from Spherical Coordinates. A unit vector \(\omega\) is then defined as: \[\omega = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\phi \sin\theta \\ \sin\phi \sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}\] Where \(\theta \in [0, \pi]\) is the polar angle and \(\phi \in [0, 2\pi)\) is the azimuthal angle.


We have \(\sin\theta\) because in z direction the arc is moving relative to \(\sin\theta\): \[d\Omega = \sin\theta d\theta d\phi\]



\[\int_{all \Omega} 1 d\Omega = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \left( \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta d\theta \right) 1 d\phi = \int_{0}^{2\pi} 2 d\phi = 4\pi\] Outer integral: \(\int_{0}^{2\pi} 1 d\phi = [\phi]_{0}^{2\pi} = 2\pi\) Inner integral: \(\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta d\theta = [-\cos\theta]_{0}^{\pi} = -\cos\pi - (-\cos0) = -(-1) - (-1) = 2\)
Therefore: \(\int_{all \Omega} 1 d\Omega = 2\pi \times 2 = 4\pi\)









Lagrangian \(L\) of a system is the difference between kinetic energy (\(T\)) and potential energy (\(V\)): \[L(x, \dot{x}) = T - V\]
If you take a path \(\vec{\mathbf{y}}(t)\) and then slightly change (or perturb) it to a nearby path, the change in the total cost (\(\delta S\)) is zero to the first order. This is exactly how you find the top or bottom of a curve in single-variable calculus: you find the point where the slope (derivative) is zero. So it is also the case here, we find the path that with slight change, the action is still the same.
We formulate it like this:



\[\forall x: \quad \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) = \frac{\partial L}{\partial x}\]
Derivation approach: \[S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} L(x, \dot{x}, t) dt\] Imagine the true path is \(x(t)\). We perturb it by a tiny amount \(\delta x(t)\). This change in the path also changes the velocity by \(\delta \dot{x}(t)\).When we calculate how much the total Action \(S\) changes (\(\delta S\)), we use the chain rule: \[\delta S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left( \frac{\partial L}{\partial x} \delta x + \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} \delta \dot{x} \right) dt = 0\]



1. Define the Lagrangian For a mass \(m\) attached to a spring with stiffness \(k\) (where \(x\) is the position and \(\dot{x}\) is the velocity):

Kinetic Energy: \(T = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2\)
Potential Energy: \(V = \frac{1}{2}kx^2\) \[L = T - V = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2 - \frac{1}{2}kx^2\]

2. Apply Euler-Lagrange Equations We have two terms in the Lagrangian:

The term with velocity: \(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} = m\dot{x}\)
The term with position: \(\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = -kx\)

Now, we plug these into the Euler-Lagrange equation: \[\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) = \frac{\partial L}{\partial x}\] \[\frac{d}{dt}(m\dot{x}) = -kx\] \[m\ddot{x} = -kx\] \[m\ddot{x} + kx = 0\] \[m\ddot{x} = -kx\]
This result, \(m\ddot{x} = -kx\), is precisely Hooke’s Law combined with Newton’s Second Law (\(F=ma\)).
So using the Principle of Stationary Action, we can derive equations of motion from the Lagrangian.




the path taken by a ray of light between two points is the path that can be traversed in the least amount of time. This principle can derive Snell’s Law & Law of Reflection.
1. Law of Reflection path of least time occurs when the angle of incidence (\(\theta_i\)) equals the angle of reflection (\(\theta_r\)). Any other path would result in a longer travel time.
2. Snell’s Law Light must travel faster in the medium with the lower refractive index. To minimize the total travel time, the light spends less time in the slower medium by bending toward the normal. \[n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2\]

Newton 2nd Law \(F = ma = m\ddot{x} = -kx\)

So Original equation is \[m\ddot{x} + kx = 0\]
Now we add \(B\dot{x}\) term (damping force) It creates a force that is proportional to the velocity (\(dx/dt\)). The faster you move, the harder the “air” pushes back against you \[m\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + kx = 0\]
Then we add actual A (unormalized actual strength unlike \(F(t) = \sin(t)\) only between -1 and 1) \[m\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + kx = A(t)\]


Idea Because the acceleration \(\ddot{x}\) is proportional to the negative position \(-x\), the solution is a sine/cosine wave. \[x(t) = A \cdot \cos(\omega t + \phi)\] \[\dot{x}(t) = -\omega A \cdot \sin(\omega t + \phi)\] \[\ddot{x}(t) = -\omega^2 A \cdot \cos(\omega t + \phi)\]
