Using Conditiona Bayes to “flip” the problem between \(\mathbf{z}_k\) and \(\mathbf{x}_k\):\[P(\mathbf{x}_k | \mathbf{z}_k, H) = \frac{P(\mathbf{z}_k | \mathbf{x}_k, H) P(\mathbf{x}_k | H)}{P(\mathbf{z}_k | H)}\]
Look at the term \(P(\mathbf{z}_k | \mathbf{x}_k, H)\). The measurement, physics of camera etc are not dependent on our history. It is true that in BA, we maximize the samt \(P(\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_k, \mathbf{y}_1, \dots, \mathbf{y}_m | \mathbf{z}_{1:k})\), but inside each frame \(z_{i,j}\) only depends on \(x_i\) and \(y_j\) : \[P(\mathbf{z}_k | \mathbf{x}_k, H) \approx P(\mathbf{z}_k | \mathbf{x}_k)\]
The denominator \(P(\mathbf{z}_k | H)\) is just the probability of seeing those specific pixels regardless of where you are. Since it doesn’t depend on our variable \(\mathbf{x}_k\), we treat it as a constant:\[P(\mathbf{x}_k | \mathbf{z}_k, H) \propto P(\mathbf{z}_k | \mathbf{x}_k) P(\mathbf{x}_k | H)\] Which is: \[P(\mathbf{x}_k | \dots) \propto \underbrace{P(\mathbf{z}_k | \mathbf{x}_k)}_{\text{Likelihood}} \underbrace{P(\mathbf{x}_k | \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{u}_{1:k}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})}_{\text{Prior}}\]

Likelihood: “How well does my current guess explain the pixels I am seeing right now?” (reprojection/photometric error in previous blog).
Prior: “What did I think my position was before I opened my eyes in this frame, based on my previous movement?”. And the prior itself is also not sure: \[P(\mathbf{x}_k | \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{u}_{1:k}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) = \int P(\mathbf{x}_k | \mathbf{x}_{k-1}, \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{u}_{1:k}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) P(\mathbf{x}_{k-1} | \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{u}_{1:k}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) d\mathbf{x}_{k-1}\]

There are 2 approaches to solve this problem:

Filtering Method (EKF) faster but less accurate
Optimization Method (Batch/Nonlinear) slower but more accurate

title: State Estimation
State Estimation
text:
Explaination:
Your question would be, how did we come from \[P(\mathbf{x}_k | \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{u}_{1:k}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) = \int P(\mathbf{x}_k | \mathbf{x}_{k-1}, \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{u}_{1:k}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) P(\mathbf{x}_{k-1} | \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{u}_{1:k}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) d\mathbf{x}_{k-1}\] to \[\begin{cases} \mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{u}_k + \mathbf{w}_k \\ \mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{C}_k \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k \end{cases} \]
Firstly, \(\mathbf{x}_k = \mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{u}_k + \mathbf{w}_k\) is the probability \(P(\mathbf{x}_k | \mathbf{x}_{k-1}, \mathbf{u}_k)\).

The Mean of the probability is \(\mathbf{A}_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1} + \mathbf{u}_k\) (where we expect to land).
The Variance (the spread of the cloud) is determined by the noise term \(\mathbf{w}_k\).

Secondly, The term \(P(\mathbf{x}_{k-1} | \mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{u}_{1:k-1}, \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1}) d\mathbf{x}_{k-1}\) represents all the result points of the previous frame. It is the bell curve (Gaussian) that the robot calculated at time \(k-1\). And you know, we dont save all points like that, we summarize them into two variables:

\(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1}\): The mean (the center of the earlier point).
\(\hat{\mathbf{P}}_{k-1}\): The covariance (how dense the distribution is).

AND EVENTUALLY, both things above are collapsed into this:

The center of the distribution: \(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1}\) (in: \(\check{\mathbf{x}}_k = \mathbf{A}_k \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k-1} + \mathbf{u}_k\) )
The width of the distribution: \(\hat{\mathbf{P}}_{k-1}\) (in: \(\check{\mathbf{P}}_k = \mathbf{A}_k \hat{\mathbf{P}}_{k-1} \mathbf{A}_k^T + \mathbf{R}\) )

title: State Estimation
text:
Camera Projection Model
To formulate error,let’s start with a 3D point \(\mathbf{p}\) becomes a 2D pixel \(\mathbf{z}\). This is the “Observation Equation” \(h(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})\) broken down into its physics steps.
Step 1: World to Camera Frame Transform the 3D point \(\mathbf{p}\) using the camera’s rotation \(\mathbf{R}\) and translation \(\mathbf{t}\):\[\mathbf{P}' = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{t} = [X', Y', Z']^T\]
Step 2: Projection to Normalized PlaneDivide by depth (\(Z'\)) to get coordinates on the virtual plane 1 meter away from the lens:\[\mathbf{P}_c = [u_c, v_c, 1]^T = [X'/Z', Y'/Z', 1]^T\]
Step 3: Distortion Distortion coefficients \(k_1, k_2\) (Note: \(r_c^2 = u_c^2 + v_c^2\) is the squared distance from the center).:\[\begin{cases} u'_c = u_c(1 + k_1r_c^2 + k_2r_c^4) \\ v'_c = v_c(1 + k_1r_c^2 + k_2r_c^4) \end{cases}\]
Step 4: Intrinsics to Pixel Coordinates Use the focal lengths (\(f_x, f_y\)) and principal point (\(c_x, c_y\)) to find the final pixel address:\[\begin{cases} u_s = f_x u'_c + c_x \\ v_s = f_y v'_c + c_y \end{cases}\]
Summarize: If we group all the camera parameters (like pose \(\mathbf{T}\) and intrinsics) as \(\mathbf{x}\) and the 3D landmark positions as \(\mathbf{y}\), we can summarize all four steps above into one function \(h\):\[\mathbf{z} = h(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})\]
In a perfect world, your camera would see the landmark exactly where \(h(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})\) says it should be. In reality, noise makes them different. We define the Reprojection Error (\(\mathbf{e}\)):\[\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{z} - h(\mathbf{T}, \mathbf{p})\]
Our goal is to minimize the sum of all squared errors:\[\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^m \sum_{j=1}^n \|\mathbf{e}_{ij}\|^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^m \sum_{j=1}^n \|\mathbf{z}_{ij} - h(\mathbf{T}_i, \mathbf{p}_j)\|^2\]

title: State Estimation
text:
Solve BA
In BA, our unknown variable \(\mathbf{x}\) is a giant vector containing every camera pose and every 3D landmark.\[\mathbf{x} = [\mathbf{T}_1, \dots, \mathbf{T}_m, \mathbf{p}_1, \dots, \mathbf{p}_n]^T\]
To solve this using Gauss-Newton, we need to find an increment \(\Delta \mathbf{x}\) that reduces the error. We split this increment into two parts:

\(\Delta \mathbf{x}_c\): The update for all \(m\) camera poses (in Lie Algebra \(\mathfrak{se}(3)\)).
\(\Delta \mathbf{x}_p\): The update for all \(n\) 3D point positions.

The cost function is the sum of all reprojection errors. We use a Taylor expansion to linearize it. For a single observation of point \(j\) by camera \(i\), the error becomes:\[\frac{1}{2} \|f(\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x})\|^2 \approx \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^m \sum_{j=1}^n \|\mathbf{e}_{ij} + \mathbf{F}_{ij}\Delta\xi_i + \mathbf{E}_{ij}\Delta\mathbf{p}_j\|^2\]

\(\mathbf{F}_{ij}\): “If I wiggle the \(i\)-th camera pose (\(\delta\xi_i\)), how much does the \(j\)-th pixel move?”
\(\mathbf{E}_{ij}\): “If I wiggle the \(j\)-th 3D point (\(\Delta \mathbf{p}_j\)), how much does its projection move on the \(i\)-th camera’s sensor?”

When we stack all these observations into one large matrix, the equation simplifies to:\[\frac{1}{2} \|f(\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x})\|^2 = \frac{1}{2} \|\mathbf{e} + \mathbf{F}\Delta\mathbf{x}_c + \mathbf{E}\Delta\mathbf{x}_p\|^2\]
To find the best Gradient and \(\Delta\mathbf{x}\), we solve the linear system derived from the Gauss-Newton or Levenberg-Marquardt method:\[\mathbf{H}\Delta\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{g}\]

\(\mathbf{H}\): The Hessian matrix (Information Matrix).
\(\mathbf{g}\): The Gradient vector.

Since we categorized the variables into poses and landmarks. We define the global Jacobian \(\mathbf{J}\) by placing the camera Jacobians and point Jacobians side-by-side:\[\mathbf{J} = [\mathbf{F} \quad \mathbf{E}]\] \[\mathbf{J} = \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|ccc} \mathbf{F}_{11} & \mathbf{0} & \dots & \mathbf{E}_{11} & \mathbf{0} & \dots \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{F}_{21} & \dots & \mathbf{E}_{21} & \dots & \dots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \end{array} \right]\]
The Hessian matrix \(\mathbf{H}\) is built by multiplying \(\mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J}\). This results in a very specific block-matrix structure:\[\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{F}^T\mathbf{F} & \mathbf{F}^T\mathbf{E} \\ \mathbf{E}^T\mathbf{F} & \mathbf{E}^T\mathbf{E} \end{bmatrix}\]

\(\mathbf{F}^T\mathbf{F}\): Interactions between cameras.
\(\mathbf{E}^T\mathbf{E}\): Interactions between landmarks.
The Off-Diagonals: The constraints that connect cameras to landmarks.

You know, H is a huge huge huge matrix. But it is mostly sparse, so in next chapter we solve uisng Schur Complement.

title: State Estimation
section: More explaination on E and F
text:
More explaination on E and F

Derivation
Main idea, we need to see how the error \(\mathbf{e}\) changes when we wiggle the landmark \(\mathbf{p}\) or the camera pose \(\mathbf{T}\).Let’s break down the projection \(h(\mathbf{T}, \mathbf{p})\) into two main layers:

Geometry Layer: The 3D point in world coordinates \(\mathbf{p}\) is moved to the camera frame \(\mathbf{P}' = [X', Y', Z']^T\).
Projection Layer: The camera-frame point \(\mathbf{P}'\) is projected into pixel coordinates \(\mathbf{u} = [u, v]^T\).



The Common Starting Component
\(\frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'}\)Both \(\mathbf{F}\) and \(\mathbf{E}\) share a middle step: How do pixel coordinates change if the 3D point inside the camera frame moves?From the projection equations \(u = f_x \frac{X'}{Z'} + c_x\) and \(v = f_y \frac{Y'}{Z'} + c_y\), we take the partial derivatives with respect to \(X', Y', Z'\):\[\frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial u}{\partial X'} & \frac{\partial u}{\partial Y'} & \frac{\partial u}{\partial Z'} \\ \frac{\partial v}{\partial X'} & \frac{\partial v}{\partial Y'} & \frac{\partial v}{\partial Z'} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{f_x}{Z'} & 0 & -\frac{f_x X'}{Z'^2} \\ 0 & \frac{f_y}{Z'} & -\frac{f_y Y'}{Z'^2} \end{bmatrix}\]


Deriving \(\mathbf{E}\) (Landmark Jacobian)
\(\mathbf{E}\) is the derivative of the error with respect to the 3D world point \(\mathbf{p}\).Since \(\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{z} - h(\mathbf{T}, \mathbf{p})\), the Jacobian is \(-\frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{p}}\).The Chain Rule:\[\mathbf{E} = - \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{P}'}{\partial \mathbf{p}}\]
We already have the first part. For the second part, since \(\mathbf{P}' = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{t}\), the derivative with respect to \(\mathbf{p}\) is simply the rotation matrix \(\mathbf{R}\).The Final Result for \(\mathbf{E}\) has size \(2 \times 3\):\[\mathbf{E} = - \begin{bmatrix} \frac{f_x}{Z'} & 0 & -\frac{f_x X'}{Z'^2} \\ 0 & \frac{f_y}{Z'} & -\frac{f_y Y'}{Z'^2} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}\]


Deriving \(\mathbf{F}\) (Camera Jacobian)
\(\mathbf{F}\) is the derivative of the error with respect to the camera pose perturbation \(\delta\xi \in \mathfrak{se}(3)\).The Chain Rule:\[\mathbf{F} = - \frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{P}'}{\partial \delta \xi}\]
From Lie Algebra, we know that if we apply a tiny left-multiplication perturbation \(\exp(\delta\xi^\wedge)\) to the transformation, the derivative of the transformed point \(\mathbf{P}'\) with respect to the perturbation is:\[\frac{\partial \mathbf{P}'}{\partial \delta \xi} = [\mathbf{I} \quad -\mathbf{P}'^\wedge] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & Z' & -Y' \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -Z' & 0 & X' \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & Y' & -X' & 0 \end{bmatrix}\]Now, multiply the two matrices together:\[\mathbf{F} = - \begin{bmatrix} \frac{f_x}{Z'} & 0 & -\frac{f_x X'}{Z'^2} \\ 0 & \frac{f_y}{Z'} & -\frac{f_y Y'}{Z'^2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & Z' & -Y' \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -Z' & 0 & X' \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & Y' & -X' & 0 \end{bmatrix}\]The Final Result for \(\mathbf{F}\):After performing the matrix multiplication, you get the \(2 \times 6\) matrix:\[\mathbf{F} = - \begin{bmatrix} \frac{f_x}{Z'} & 0 & -\frac{f_x X'}{Z'^2} & -\frac{f_x X' Y'}{Z'^2} & f_x + \frac{f_x X'^2}{Z'^2} & -\frac{f_x Y'}{Z'} \\ 0 & \frac{f_y}{Z'} & -\frac{f_y Y'}{Z'^2} & -f_y - \frac{f_y Y'^2}{Z'^2} & \frac{f_y X' Y'}{Z'^2} & \frac{f_y X'}{Z'} \end{bmatrix}\]


Example

Camera Intrinsics (\(\mathbf{K}\)): \(f_x = 500, f_y = 500, c_x = 320, c_y = 240\).
3D Point (\(\mathbf{p}\)): \([1, 1, 5]^T\) in the camera frame. (So \(X'=1, Y'=1, Z'=5\)).
Camera Pose (\(\mathbf{T}\)): For simplicity, we’ll assume the camera is at the world origin with no rotation. (So \(\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}\) and \(\mathbf{t} = [0,0,0]^T\)).



Step 1: The Intermediate Projection Jacobian
First, we calculate \(\frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'}\). This tells us how the pixels \((u, v)\) react to 3D movement in the camera frame.Using the formulas:

\(\frac{f_x}{Z'} = \frac{500}{5} = \mathbf{100}\)
\(-\frac{f_x X'}{Z'^2} = -\frac{500 \cdot 1}{25} = \mathbf{-20}\)
\(\frac{f_y}{Z'} = \frac{500}{5} = \mathbf{100}\)
\(-\frac{f_y Y'}{Z'^2} = -\frac{500 \cdot 1}{25} = \mathbf{-20}\) \[\frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 0 & -20 \\ 0 & 100 & -20 \end{bmatrix}\]



Step 3: Calculate the Camera Jacobian (\(\mathbf{F}\))
We use the formula \(\mathbf{F} = -\frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial \mathbf{P}'} [\mathbf{I}, -\mathbf{P}'^\wedge]\).First, let’s find the skew-symmetric matrix \(-\mathbf{P}'^\wedge\):\[-\mathbf{P}'^\wedge = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & Z' & -Y' \\ -Z' & 0 & X' \\ Y' & -X' & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & -1 \\ -5 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\]
Now we multiply: \[\mathbf{F} = - \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 0 & -20 \\ 0 & 100 & -20 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 5 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & -5 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\]
After the matrix multiplication, we get: \[\mathbf{F} = \begin{bmatrix} -100 & 0 & 20 & 20 & -520 & 100 \\ 0 & -100 & 20 & 520 & -20 & -100 \end{bmatrix}\]
The first 3 columns are translation. The last 3 are rotation. Notice how rotation (\(520\)) has a much bigger impact on the pixels than translation (\(-100\))? This is why camera “shake” ruins photos so easily!


Step 4: Putting them together
\[\mathbf{J} = \left[ \begin{array}{ccc|ccc} \mathbf{F}_{11} & \mathbf{0} & \dots & \mathbf{E}_{11} & \mathbf{0} & \dots \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{F}_{21} & \dots & \mathbf{E}_{21} & \dots & \dots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \end{array} \right]\]

Setup

Cameras (\(m=3\)): Each has 6 degrees of freedom (Lie Algebra \(\xi \in \mathfrak{se}(3)\)).
Points (\(n=5\)): Each has 3 degrees of freedom (\(X, Y, Z\)).
Visibility: Let’s assume a realistic scenario where not every camera sees every point:

Cam 1 sees Points 1, 2, 3. (3 observations)
Cam 2 sees Points 2, 3, 4. (3 observations)
Cam 3 sees Points 3, 4, 5. (3 observations)

Total Observations: 9 pairs of (Camera \(i\), Point \(j\)).



Dimensions of the “Mega” Matrix \(J\)
The columns correspond to our variables. We have a “Camera section” and a “Landmark section.” * Camera Columns: \(3 \text{ cameras} \times 6 \text{ DOF} = \mathbf{18 \text{ columns}}\). * Landmark Columns: \(5 \text{ points} \times 3 \text{ DOF} = \mathbf{15 \text{ columns}}\). * Total Width of \(J\): 33 columns.
The rows correspond to our errors. Each observation \(z_{ij}\) produces a 2D residual (error in \(u\) and error in \(v\)). * Total Rows: \(9 \text{ observations} \times 2 \text{ residuals} = \mathbf{18 \text{ rows}}\).
=> Total Size of \(J\) for this example: \(18 \times 33\).


The Global Jacobian \(\mathbf{J}\)
This matrix has 18 rows (9 observations \(\times\) 2 pixels) and 33 columns (18 for poses + 15 for points). \[\mathbf{J} =
\left[
\begin{array}{ccc|ccccc}
\mathbf{F}_{11} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{E}_{11} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\
\mathbf{F}_{12} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{E}_{12} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\
\mathbf{F}_{13} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{E}_{13} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\ \hline
\mathbf{0} & \mathbf{F}_{22} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{E}_{22} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\
\mathbf{0} & \mathbf{F}_{23} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{E}_{23} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\
\mathbf{0} & \mathbf{F}_{24} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{E}_{24} & \mathbf{0} \\ \hline
\mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{F}_{33} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{E}_{33} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} \\
\mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{F}_{34} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{E}_{34} & \mathbf{0} \\
\mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{F}_{35} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{E}_{35}
\end{array}
\right]\]
Pixel resolution does not change anything. It is only about the number of features detected, and the number of camera poses.

title: Kalman Filter
text:
Before we calculate anything, we define how the world works using 2 things:

State Transition Equation: \(x_k = \Phi x_{k-1} + w_k\)

\(x_k\): The true state (e.g., position and velocity) at time \(k\).
\(\Phi\) (or \(F\)): The matrix that moves the state forward in time (the “physics”).
\(w_k\): Process Noise (variance \(Q\)) (like the wind?)

Observation Equation: \(z_k = H x_k + v_k\)

\(z_k\): The actual measurement from the sensor. (what sensor sees)
\(H\): The matrix that maps the state to what the sensor “sees.”
\(v_k\): Measurement Noise (variance \(R\)).





Project the State:\[\hat{x}_{k|k-1} = \Phi \hat{x}_{k-1}\]
Project the Error Covariance:\[P_{k|k-1} = \Phi P_{k-1} \Phi^T + Q\]



Calculate Kalman Gain (\(K\)): (how much we trust the sensor measurement = how much we untrust the prediction) \[K_k = P_{k|k-1} H^T (H P_{k|k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}\]
Update State with the trust of sensor measurement: \[\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k|k-1})\]
Decrease the Uncertainty: \[P_k = (I - K_k H) P_{k|k-1}\]

title: Kalman Filter
text:
Before we calculate anything, we define how the world works using 2 things:

State Transition Equation: \(x_k = \Phi x_{k-1} + w_k\)

\(x_k\): The true state (e.g., position and velocity) at time \(k\).
\(\Phi\) (or \(F\)): The matrix that moves the state forward in time (the “physics”).
\(w_k\): Process Noise (variance \(Q\)) (like the wind?)

Observation Equation: \(z_k = H x_k + v_k\)

\(z_k\): The actual measurement from the sensor. (what sensor sees)
\(H\): The matrix that maps the state to what the sensor “sees.”
\(v_k\): Measurement Noise (variance \(R\)).

title: Kalman Filter
text:
Project the State:\[\hat{x}_{k|k-1} = \Phi \hat{x}_{k-1}\]
Project the Error Covariance:\[P_{k|k-1} = \Phi P_{k-1} \Phi^T + Q\]

title: Kalman Filter
text:
Calculate Kalman Gain (\(K\)): (how much we trust the sensor measurement = how much we untrust the prediction) \[K_k = P_{k|k-1} H^T (H P_{k|k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}\]
Update State with the trust of sensor measurement: \[\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k|k-1})\]
Decrease the Uncertainty: \[P_k = (I - K_k H) P_{k|k-1}\]

title: Kalman Filter
text:
System Setup (Model Assumption)
Imagine a car moving along a straight line. We check its status every \(\Delta t = 1\) second.
State Vector: \(\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} s_k \\ v_k \end{bmatrix}\) (Position, Velocity)
State Transition Matrix (\(\Phi\)) in \(x_k = \Phi x_{k-1} + w_k\): (\(s_{new} = s_{old} + v \cdot \Delta t\) and \(v_{new} = v_{old}\)): \[\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\]
Observation Matrix (\(H\)) in \(z_k = H x_k + v_k\): Our GPS measures directly only position, so 1 for that: \[H = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\]
Noise: Let’s assume Measurement Noise \(R = 4\) and Process Noise \(Q = 0\) (perfect physics).

title: Kalman Filter
text:
Initial Guess
\(\mathbf{\hat{x}}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}\) and \(P_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\)

title: Kalman Filter
text:
Prediction Step (Time = 1s)
Before looking at the GPS, we predict: \[\mathbf{\hat{x}}_{1|0} = \Phi \mathbf{\hat{x}}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{\begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix}}\] \[P_{1|0} = \Phi P_0 \Phi^T = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\]

title: Kalman Filter
text:
Update Step (Time = 1s)
Now we get the GPS measurement \(z_1 = 13\) (eventhough we predicted 10 :)))
Calculate Kalman Gain (trust of sensor measurement = how much we untrust the prediction): \[K_1 = P_{1|0} H^T (H P_{1|0} H^T + R)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} (\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 4)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} (2 + 4)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2/6 \\ 1/6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.333 \\ 0.167 \end{bmatrix}\]
Update State with the trust of sensor measurement: \[\mathbf{\hat{x}}_1 = \mathbf{\hat{x}}_{1|0} + K_1 (z_1 - H \mathbf{\hat{x}}_{1|0}) = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.333 \\ 0.167 \end{bmatrix} (13 - 10) = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 10 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{\begin{bmatrix} 11 \\ 10.5 \end{bmatrix}}\]
Decrease the Uncertainty: \[P_1 = (I - K_1 H) P_{1|0} = (\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0.333 & 0 \\ 0.167 & 0 \end{bmatrix}) \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.667 & 1 \\ -0.167 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.333 & 0.667 \\ 0.833 & 0.833 \end{bmatrix}\]

title: Kalman Filter
text:
Simplest Static Example
Firstly the difference:

MSE says: “I used \(Y\) once in the lab to find \(a=1.214\). I will use \(1.214\) forever.”
Kalman says: “I used the statistics of \(Y\) (its variance) to set my initial trust. But as I see more \(X\) values, I will look at the ‘surprises’ (innovations) to refine my guess in real-time.”


The Static MSE Approach

You use the Ground Truth \(Y\) in the lab to find a fixed weight \(a\). You never change that weight and use it to estimate \(Y\) from \(X\) in real world.

What we know from the World Model: true temperature is constant \(x = 20\) (or manufacturer etc…)
Measurements: \(z_1 = 18\), \(z_2 = 22\), \(z_3 = 19\). We usually solve using \(C_{YX} = a C_X\). Now let’s calculate the Covariances (\(C_X\) and \(C_{YX}\)):
\[E\{X_1^2\} = \frac{18^2 + 22^2 + 19^2}{3} = \frac{1161}{3} = 387\] \[E\{Y X_1\} = \frac{(20\cdot18) + (20\cdot22) + (20\cdot19)}{3} = \frac{1180}{3}\]
Solution \(a = \frac{E\{YX_1\}}{E\{X_1^2\}} = \frac{1180/3}{387}\) \[\hat{Y} = \frac{1}{3}(a X_1 + a X_2 + a X_3) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1180}{387 \cdot 3} \cdot (18 + 22 + 19) \approx 19.9885\]


Static Kalman Filter

“I don’t know the truth \(Y\), but I will keep collecting data until trust in data \(P\) is more than trust in sensor noise \(\sigma_v^2\). Because it’s when we say ‘I trust my filter more than the next measurement’. And \(P_0\) is the initial trust. and \(\hat{x}_0\) is the initial guess.”

What we know from the World Model: this time not the value \(x=20\) but the statistics. Sensor with noise variance \(\sigma_v^2 = 4\).We start with a “Prior Belief” (initial guess) of \(\hat{x}_0 = 25\) with an initial uncertainty (error variance) of \(P_0 = 10\).
Step 1: \(z_1 = 18\): \[K_1 = \frac{P_0}{P_0 + \sigma_v^2} = \frac{10}{10 + 4} = \mathbf{0.714}\] \[\hat{x}_1 = \hat{x}_0 + K_1(z_1 - \hat{x}_0) = 25 + 0.714(18 - 25) = \mathbf{20}\] \[P_1 = (1 - K_1)P_0 = (1 - 0.714) \times 10 = \mathbf{2.86}\]
Step 2: \(z_2 = 22\): \[K_2 = \frac{P_1}{P_1 + \sigma_v^2} = \frac{2.86}{2.86 + 4} = \mathbf{0.417}\] \[\hat{x}_2 = \hat{x}_1 + K_2(z_2 - \hat{x}_1) = 20 + 0.417(22 - 20) = \mathbf{20.83}\] \[P_2 = (1 - K_2)P_1 = (1 - 0.417) \times 2.86 = \mathbf{1.67}\]
We keep measuring until \(P_n\) is smaller than sensor noise \(\sigma_v^2\). Because it’s when we say “I trust my filter more than the next measurement”.

title: Kalman Filter
text:
Dynamic Example
Increasing temperature

True State (\(x_t\)): Starts at 20°C and increases: \(x_t = x_{t-1} + 1\).
Process Noise (\(Q\)): We are 100% sure about this “+1” rule, so \(Q = 0\).
Sensor Noise (\(\sigma_v^2\)): Still 4.
Initial Guess: \(\hat{x}_0 = 20\) with uncertainty \(P_0 = 2\).

Step 1: (Sensor measured \(z_1 = 22.5\)):

Predicted State: \(\hat{x}_{1|0} = 20 + 1 = \mathbf{21}\)
Predicted Uncertainty: \(P_{1|0} = P_0 + Q = 2 + 0 = \mathbf{2}\)
Kalman Gain (how much we trust the sensor): \(K_1 = \frac{P_{1|0}}{P_{1|0} + \sigma_v^2} = \frac{2}{2 + 4} = \mathbf{0.333}\)
Updated State: \(\hat{x}_1 = \hat{x}_{1|0} + K_1(z_1 - \hat{x}_{1|0}) = 21 + 0.333(22.5 - 21) = \mathbf{21.5}\)
Updated Uncertainty: \(P_1 = (1 - K_1)P_{1|0} = (1 - 0.333) \times 2 = \mathbf{1.334}\)

Step 2: (Sensor measured \(z_2 = 21\))

Predicted State: \(\hat{x}_{2|1} = \hat{x}_1 + 1 = 21.5 + 1 = \mathbf{22.5}\)
Predicted Uncertainty: \(P_{2|1} = P_1 + Q = 1.334 + 0 = \mathbf{1.334}\)
Kalman Gain: \(K_2 = \frac{P_{2|1}}{P_{2|1} + \sigma_v^2} = \frac{1.334}{1.334 + 4} = \mathbf{0.249}\)
Updated State: \(\hat{x}_2 = \hat{x}_{2|1} + K_2(z_2 - \hat{x}_{2|1}) = 22.5 + 0.249(21 - 22.5) = \mathbf{22.126}\)
Updated Uncertainty: \(P_2 = (1 - K_2)P_{2|1} = (1 - 0.249) \times 1.334 = \mathbf{1.002}\)

title: Exam Prediction & Estimation Techniques
text:
Random Variables

We know a random variable is normal with mean \(\eta\) and standard deviation \(\sigma\). If \(f(\eta) = 0.6\), what is its variance?
Answer: Normal distribution pdf: \[f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\eta}{\sigma}\right)^2}\] we have: \[f(\eta) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} = 0.6\] \[\sigma = \frac{1}{0.6 \sqrt{2\pi}}\]


Estimate \(\mathbf{x}\) using two random variables \(\mathbf{x_1}\) and \(\mathbf{x_2}\). How does a simple linear model look?
Answer: The general affine (linear with an offset) model looks like this: \[\hat{x} = a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2 + b\]


The random variable \(\mathbf{x}\) is exponential with \(f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x} U(x)\). Determine its conditional density \(f(x|M)\) for \(M = (\mathbf{x} \ge x_0)\).
Answer: Let’s start with this: \[f(x|M) = \frac{f(x, M)}{P(M)}\] But what is \(f(x, M)\)? Actually \(f(x, M)\) is still simply the original curve \(f(x)\), but chopped off at \(x_0\) (so that everything before \(x_0\) is completely erased (set to zero)). But now we have to scale it back! So we divide by \(P(M)\) to make the total area under the curve equal to 1. To find \(P(M)\) we integrate \(f(x)\) from \(x_0\) to infinity: \[P(M) = P(\mathbf{x} \ge x_0) = \int_{x_0}^{\infty} \lambda e^{-\lambda x} dx = e^{-\lambda x_0}\] Therefore: \[f(x|M) = \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda x}}{e^{-\lambda x_0}} = \lambda e^{-\lambda (x - x_0)}\] Basically it looks exactly like original curve but shifted to the left by \(x_0\).



Function of ONE Random Variable

Find the density of \(\mathbf{y}\) given \(\mathbf{y} = 3\mathbf{x}^2\), using direct substitution and the Jacobian approach. Assume the input has a density \(f_x(x)\).
Answer: For a given y, we can have multiple x values, so we need to sum over all possible x values: \[f_y(y) = \sum f_x(x_i) |J_i|\] where Jacobian \(J\) is simply the derivative of the inverse transformation: \(J = \frac{dx}{dy}\). It is quite intuitive right? \[f_y(y) = f_x(x_1)|J_1| + f_x(x_2)|J_2|\] \[f_y(y) = f_x(\sqrt{y/3}) \left( \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3y}} \right) + f_x(-\sqrt{y/3}) \left( \frac{1}{2\sqrt{3y}} \right)\]


A zero-mean normal distribution goes through a ReLU function. What is the output distribution?
Answer: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) function is defined as: \(y = \max(0, x)\). \[f_y(y) = \sum f_x(x_i) |J_i|\] with \(J = \frac{dx}{dy}\). For \(y > 0\), we have \(x = y\), so \(J = 1\). For \(y < 0\), we have \(x = 0\), so \(J = 0\). Therefore: \[f_y(y) = \sum_{y>0} f_x(y) + \sum_{y<0} f_x(0) \cdot 0\] \[f_y(y) = \begin{cases} f_x(y) & y > 0 \\ 0 & y < 0 \end{cases}\]


A hidden layer uses \(y = \tanh(x)\). If the input has density \(f(x)\), what is the density of \(y\)?
\[f_y(y) = \sum f_x(x_i) |J_i|\] with \(J = \frac{dx}{dy}\). Since \(y = \tanh(x)\), we have \(x = \text{arctanh}(y)\). \[f_y(y) = f_x(\text{arctanh}(y)) \left| \frac{d}{dy} \text{arctanh}(y) \right| = f_x(\text{arctanh}(y)) \frac{1}{1-y^2}\]


\(\mathbf{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf{x}_i + b\). Inputs are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) with mean \(\eta\). What is the mean and variance of \(\mathbf{x}\)?
Let’s assume the variance of the individual inputs \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is \(\sigma^2\) (since they are identically distributed).
Finding the Mean (Expected Value): Expectation is a linear operator, meaning we can distribute it across sums. \[E[\mathbf{x}] = E\left[ \frac{1}{n}\sum \mathbf{x}_i + b \right]\] \[E[\mathbf{x}] = \frac{1}{n} \sum E[\mathbf{x}_i] + E[b]\] \[E[\mathbf{x}] = \frac{1}{n} (n \cdot \eta) + b\] Mean of \(\mathbf{x}\) = \(\eta + b\)
Finding the Variance: Variance has two important rules:

Adding a constant (\(b\)) shifts the data but does not change the spread, so \(\text{Var}(X + b) = \text{Var}(X)\).
Multiplying by a constant (\(1/n\)) scales the variance by the square of that constant: \(\text{Var}(cX) = c^2 \text{Var}(X)\).

In this case: \[\text{Var}(\mathbf{x}) = \text{Var}\left( \frac{1}{n}\sum \mathbf{x}_i + b \right)\] \[\text{Var}(\mathbf{x}) = \text{Var}\left( \frac{1}{n}\sum \mathbf{x}_i \right)\] \[\text{Var}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n^2} \text{Var}\left( \sum \mathbf{x}_i \right)\] because these are totally different random variables \(\mathbf{x}_i\), so it is not like we are scaling (multiply) the same variable \(n\) times. Instead, we are summing up \(n\) different variables, so the variance adds up: \[\text{Var}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n^2} (n \cdot \sigma^2) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\]
Variance of \(\mathbf{x}\) = \(\frac{\sigma^2}{n}\)


Suppose \(\mathbf{x}\) and \(\mathbf{y}\) have a one-to-one relationship. If we know \(F(x) = P\{\mathbf{x} \le x\}\), what will be \(F(y) = P\{\mathbf{y} \le y\}\)? Explain. Is it possible to establish the relationship between the two random variables if we know their distribution functions?
Let the one-to-one relationship be \(y = g(x)\). If \(g(x)\) is strictly increasing:\[F_y(y) = P(\mathbf{y} \le y) = P(g(\mathbf{x}) \le y) = P(\mathbf{x} \le g^{-1}(y)) = F_x(g^{-1}(y))\] If \(g(x)\) is strictly decreasing:\[F_y(y) = P(\mathbf{y} \le y) = P(g(\mathbf{x}) \le y) = P(\mathbf{x} \ge g^{-1}(y)) = 1 - F_x(g^{-1}(y))\]

Can we establish the relationship if we only know \(F_x(x)\) and \(F_y(y)\)?
Pretty nicely yes. If we assume they have a strictly increasing relationship, then the cumulative probabilities must match. The 50th percentile of \(\mathbf{x}\) will map to the 50th percentile of \(\mathbf{y}\).\[F_y(y) = F_x(x)\] To find the relationship \(y = g(x)\), we just apply the inverse CDF of \(y\):\[y = F_y^{-1}(F_x(x))\]




MSE

The Principle of Minimum Mean Square Estimation (MMSE)
The Cost Function: \(J = E[e^2] = E[(y - \hat{y})^2]\). We want to find the formula for \(\hat{y}\) that minimizes \(J\).
‘need correction’


We have two sensors, \(\mathbf{s}_1\) and \(\mathbf{s}_2\), and an unknown target \(\mathbf{y}\). How do we linearly combine them, and how do we find the optimal coefficients?
We assume a linear estimator (a weighted sum of the sensors). Let’s call the weights \(a_1\) and \(a_2\):\[\hat{y} = a_1 \mathbf{s}_1 + a_2 \mathbf{s}_2\]
Our error is \(e = \mathbf{y} - (a_1 \mathbf{s}_1 + a_2 \mathbf{s}_2)\). We want to minimize \(E[e^2]\). To find the minimum, we take the partial derivative of \(E[e^2]\) with respect to each weight and set them to \(0\).
Step 1: Derivative with respect to \(a_1\) \[\frac{\partial}{\partial a_1} E[(\mathbf{y} - a_1 \mathbf{s}_1 - a_2 \mathbf{s}_2)^2] = 0\] \[E[2(\mathbf{y} - a_1 \mathbf{s}_1 - a_2 \mathbf{s}_2)(-\mathbf{s}_1)] = 0\] \[E[\mathbf{y}\mathbf{s}_1 - a_1 \mathbf{s}_1^2 - a_2 \mathbf{s}_1 \mathbf{s}_2] = 0\] \[E[\mathbf{y}\mathbf{s}_1] = a_1 E[\mathbf{s}_1^2] + a_2 E[\mathbf{s}_1 \mathbf{s}_2]\]
Step 2: Derivative with respect to \(a_2\) Following the exact same steps, but taking the derivative with respect to \(a_2\), yields:\[E[\mathbf{y}\mathbf{s}_2] = a_1 E[\mathbf{s}_1 \mathbf{s}_2] + a_2 E[\mathbf{s}_2^2]\]
Step 3: Determine the Coefficients You now have a system of two linear equations. We can write this elegantly in matrix form:\[\begin{bmatrix} E[\mathbf{s}_1^2] & E[\mathbf{s}_1 \mathbf{s}_2] \\ E[\mathbf{s}_1 \mathbf{s}_2] & E[\mathbf{s}_2^2] \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} E[\mathbf{y}\mathbf{s}_1] \\ E[\mathbf{y}\mathbf{s}_2] \end{bmatrix}\]To find the optimal coefficients (\(a_1, a_2\)), you simply invert that 2x2 matrix on the left and multiply it by the vector on the right. \[a = C_{SS}^{-1} C_{Sy}\]


in MSE, the error is orthogonal to the input. Show that the error \(\mathbf{e}\) is orthogonal to the data \(\mathbf{x}\), meaning \(E(\mathbf{e}\mathbf{x}) = 0\)
This is arguably the most beautiful proof in signal processing. Let’s represent our data as a vector \(\mathbf{x}\) and our weights as a vector \(\mathbf{a}\). * Our estimate is \(\hat{y} = \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{x}\). * Our error is \(\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{x}\).
We want to find the optimal \(\mathbf{a}\) that minimizes \(J = E[\mathbf{e}^2]\). To do this, we take the gradient of \(J\) with respect to the weight vector \(\mathbf{a}\) and set it to a vector of zeros: \[\nabla_\mathbf{a} E[\mathbf{e}^2] = 0\] Move the gradient inside the expectation: \[E[\nabla_\mathbf{a} (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{x})^2] = 0\] \[E[2(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{x})(-\mathbf{x})] = 0\] \[E[(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{x})\mathbf{x}] = 0\] But wait! What is \((\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{x})\)? That is exactly our definition of the error \(\mathbf{e}\)! Substitute \(\mathbf{e}\) back in: \[E[\mathbf{e}\mathbf{x}] = 0\]

But what does this even mean?
Linear regression, the error \(y - \hat{y}\) is always orthogonal to the input \(x\). Remember?
